metadata: The Fimiston Open Pit, widely known as the Super Pit, is a large open-pit gold mine on the south-eastern edge of Kalgoorlie in Western Australia, adjacent to the Goldfields Highway. Once Australia’s largest open-cut gold mine, it was surpassed in scale in 2015 by the Newmont Boddington operation. The pit is oblong in plan, approximately 3.5 kilometres long, 1.6 kilometres wide, and more than 675 metres deep. Now owned by Northern Star Resources, the mine produced about 456,000 troy ounces of gold in 2019 and directly employs roughly 1,100 workers and contractors. Geologically, the Super Pit lies within the famous “Golden Mile,” an area named for its exceptionally rich concentration of gold deposits. Mineralisation occurs in ore lodes associated with ancient shear zones within the Golden Mile Dolerite, extending over more than two kilometres in length and one kilometre in depth. Gold here is unusual because it commonly occurs as telluride minerals within pyrite. Mining is conducted using conventional drill-and-blast methods with large shovels and haul trucks, moving around 15 million tonnes of rock annually, most of which is waste. Ore processing involves crushing, gravity separation, flotation, and ultrafine grinding, followed by cyanide extraction either on site or at the Gidji Processing Plant north of Kalgoorlie. Beyond production, the Super Pit has generated significant controversy. Environmental and health concerns include dust emissions containing heavy metals, potential water contamination, and noise and vibration from blasting. Worker safety issues, such as mercury exposure and serious injuries linked to management failures, have attracted regulatory scrutiny. Community debates persist over mine expansion, Fly-In Fly-Out accommodation, declining property values, and perceptions of limited transparency and regulatory effectiveness.

Site: Collahuasi Mining District, Pica, Tamarugal Province, Tarapacá
metadata: Quebrada Blanca is a copper mine located in the Atacama Desert of northern Chile at an elevation of 4’400 meters, approximately 1’500 kilometers from Santiago. Copper concentrate processed at the site is transported to the port facility for loading onto ship for transport to market. Quebrada Blanca is majority owned by Teck Resources and uses desalinated seawater for all production processes. In 2023 the mine produced 64’300 tons of copper.

Balmertown is a community within the Municipality of Red Lake, located in the Red Lake gold district, Kenora District, Ontario, Canada, on the traditional lands of the Wabauskang and Lac Seul First Nations. It's part of a region known for its high-grade gold deposits, particularly within the Balmer Assemblage of Archean rocks. The Red Lake gold district  and its underground mines are being restored and expanded in 2025.

In the early 20th century, the Crowsnest Pass Coal mines in the Canadian Rockies were home to languages and cultures of European immigrants. Through the years, coal mining suffered from fluctuating coal prices, bitter strikes, and underground accidents. The mines are now silent symbols of generations of workers and their families, and the changing fortunes of the mining industry. A recent study found that mountaintop removal coal mining such as that practiced in Crowsnest increases the discharge of mine effluent to rivers, underscoring the lasting downstream impacts of abandoned and even reclaimed coal mines.

The Kings Mountain Mine is a large open-pit mine with over 100 different mineral specimens on the south side of Kings Mountain in Cleveland County, North Carolina. Its open-pit has been excavated in one of the largest bedrock lithium deposits in the United States. Albemarle has started the permitting process to resume open pit mining and expand the old gold mine,idled in the early 1990s.

The history of the Beauvoir quarry reaches all the way back to the bronze age. In the 19th century the mineral Kaolin was first found on site and used for ceramics. Kaolin mining was industrialized in the early 21st century. Already in the 1960s granite bearing lithium was identified, and more recently field studies found economically significant deposits of lithium in the mine area.

The Kalongwe copper-cobalt project, located near Mutshatsha about 60 km southwest of Kolwezi in Lualaba Province, DRC, lies on the western Central African Copper Belt and hosts deeply weathered, sediment-hosted, high-grade oxide mineralization. As of April 2018, it contained 7.99 Mt of proven and probable reserves grading 2.94% copper and 0.34% cobalt. Conventional open-pit mining with drilling, blasting, and truck-shovel operations will be employed, using 90 t excavators, 40 t articulated dump trucks, and a top-hammer drill rig, positioning Kalongwe as a significant source of near-surface copper and cobalt resources.

The Kalongwe copper and cobalt project, located near Mutshatsha about 60 kilometres southwest of Kolwezi in Lualaba Province of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, lies on the western Central African Copper Belt and hosts deeply weathered, sediment-hosted, high-grade oxide mineralisation. As of April 2018, it contained 7.99 million tonnes of proven and probable reserves grading 2.94 percent copper and 0.34 percent cobalt. Conventional open-pit mining with drilling, blasting, and truck-shovel operations will be employed, using 90-tonne excavators, 40-tonne articulated dump trucks, and a top-hammer drill rig, making Kalongwe a significant source of near-surface copper and cobalt resources.

The Kolwezi mine district is one of the world’s most important copper and cobalt mining areas, centred on the city of Kolwezi in southeastern Democratic Republic of the Congo. Mining in the area began as open-cut operations in 1903, building on much older local extraction of copper minerals, notably malachite, for which Kolwezi is famous among mineral collectors. The city developed rapidly after the establishment of Union Minière du Haut Katanga in 1906 and became a major industrial mining centre. Today, Kolwezi, the capital of Lualaba Province, has a population of about 673,000 and remains central to the national economy, in which mining accounts for nearly nine-tenths of export earnings. Geologically, Kolwezi lies within the Katanga Copperbelt, one of the richest metallogenic provinces in the world. Mineralisation includes copper and cobalt as well as zinc, tin (cassiterite), manganese, uranium, gold, and platinum-group elements. Copper–cobalt ores commonly occur in sedimentary and weathered deposits, with secondary copper minerals such as malachite widely developed near the surface. The extraordinary concentration and accessibility of these ores has driven both large-scale mechanised mining and extensive artisanal extraction. Despite its economic importance, mining in Kolwezi is highly controversial. The cobalt industry has been described as a socio-ecological disaster, marked by unsafe working conditions, environmental pollution, and public health impacts. In neighbourhoods such as Kasulo, artisanal miners excavated unstable pits beneath houses, leading to fatal collapses in 2014. Subsequent efforts by authorities and companies, notably Congo Dongfang Mining, a subsidiary of Huayou Cobalt, to formalise artisanal mining involved forced displacement, disputed compensation, and monopolisation of production. Studies have documented elevated metal concentrations in residents’ bodies, while reports by journalists and human rights organisations highlight ongoing concerns about labour abuse, environmental contamination, and unequal distribution of mining benefits.

The Tanco Mine at Bernic Lake in the Lac du Bonnet area of Manitoba, Canada, is a world-class source of tantalum, lithium and caesium from the Tanco Pegmatite. This 2.67-billion-year-old, highly differentiated and strongly zoned pegmatite was first discovered in 1930 and later mined for tin, beryl, lithium, tantalum and caesium at various periods. Its pollucite deposit, the largest known globally, contains about 350,000 tons of caesium—roughly two-thirds of the world’s identified resources. Operations passed from The Tantalum Mining Corporation of Canada to Cabot Corporation in 1993 and to Sinomine in 2019, which continues caesium brine production.

The Foote Lithium Co. Mine is nestled in Kings Mountain in Cleveland County, North Carolina. This location is part of the Kings Mountain Mining District, historically significant for minerals like gold, lithium, and mica.  The mine is renowned for its lithium, extracted mainly from spodumene, and it boasts an impressive array of over 160 valid minerals, with 16 being type localities. It is famous for a variety of rare phosphates and silicates. The Foote Mine and its operations are now under the ownership of the Albemarle Corporation.

The Koktokay No. 3 pegmatite in Xinjiang, China, is a highly evolved lithium-beryllium-niobium-tantalum-cesium granitic pegmatite of the spodumene subtype and the largest rare-metal deposit in the Chinese Altai orogenic belt. It displays a distinctive concentric zonation with nine mineralogical-textural zones from a graphic pegmatite rim to a quartz-microcline core. Its cupola-shaped section measures about 250 meters in each dimension. Historically worked for pollucite, spodumene, lepidolite, beryl and columbite-tantalite, the deposit exemplifies an exceptional pegmatitic system with significant rare-metal resources and a well-preserved internal structure.

The Buranga pegmatite in Muhororo, Ngororero District, Western Province of Rwanda, is an unusual aluminium-rich and iron-manganese-rich phosphate pegmatite within a region known for lithium-caesium-tantalum deposits. Set amid the scenic hills of western Rwanda, it represents an important source of strategic minerals. Ongoing work focuses on defining resources and improving recovery rates of lithium, kaolin, silica and mica as part of an expanding feasibility study, highlighting the deposit’s potential for contributing to the country’s growing rare-metal and industrial mineral sector.

The Aidyrlya gold deposit, situated along the Aidyrlya River in Orenburg Oblast, Russia, lies within the Ural River basin via its tributary, the Suunduk River. This locality is recognised for its diverse mineralogy, including members of the serpentine subgroup, and is notable for exploitable or exploited commodities such as gold and tungsten. Its geological setting and resource profile make it an important site for studying mineralisation patterns and evaluating the economic potential of precious and strategic metals within the region.

The Mirny Mine in Mirny, Sakha, Russia, is a diamond-producing kimberlite deposit originally developed as a 525-meter-deep open pit with a diameter of 1,200 meters, making it the second-largest excavated hole in the world. Discovered in 1955, open-pit operations ceased in 2001, after which mining continued underground. By 1999, all production was underground, with a current capacity of 500 thousand tons of ore annually and a design capacity of one million tons per year. The mine’s projected lifespan extends to 2043, supporting its status as a significant source of diamonds in Russia.

The Bisha Mine, located 150 kilometres west of Asmara in Eritrea’s Gash Barka Region, is a large open-pit volcanic-associated massive sulfide deposit producing copper, zinc, gold, and silver. Covering a licensed mining and exploration area of 110 square kilometres, it contains reserves of 3.09 million tonnes of zinc and 800,000 tonnes of copper. The project is operated jointly by Zijin Mining, holding 55 percent, and Eritrean National Mining, holding 45 percent. Its high-grade ore and strategic metal content make Bisha a key contributor to Eritrea’s mining sector and regional mineral production.

The Imouraren uranium mine, located about 80 kilometres south of Arlit in Niger’s Agadez Region, hosts one of the country’s largest uranium reserves, with 109.1 million tonnes of ore grading 0.06 percent uranium. The sandstone-hosted deposit was developed by French company Areva and SOPaMin on behalf of the Nigerien government. Excavations began in 2012 following a 2009 operating permit but were suspended in 2015 due to low uranium prices. In 2023, Orano explored in-situ recovery methods for extraction, raising concerns over potential impacts on local water resources.

The Houcao bauxite deposit in Bozhou District, Zunyi, Guizhou, China, forms part of the Lower Carboniferous Jiujialu Formation and consists of thick, cyclic layers of bauxite ore and bauxitic claystone. Developed through intense chemical weathering and leaching, the deposit is rich in aluminum oxide and the mineral diaspore, with low silica content, reflecting formation in a well-drained environment. As the primary source of metallic aluminum, bauxite from Houcao is a vital strategic mineral, essential for aluminum production, supporting industrial development, and underpinning the supply of this key metal for manufacturing and infrastructure.

The Tsumeb Mine in Namibia is a world-renowned copper, lead, zinc, silver, germanium, and cadmium deposit, noted for its exceptional mineral diversity, with at least 170 species recorded, including 20 unique to the locality. Formation occurred in a non-arid environment with oxygen-rich groundwater, promoting leaching and redeposition of elements into crystalline structures. The nearby metal smelter, historically operated by Namibia Custom Smelters and sold by Weatherly International to Dundee Corporation in 2010, has contributed to significant soil contamination with arsenic and other heavy metals, highlighting both the site’s economic importance and environmental challenges

The Koashva Open Pit on Koashva Mountain, Khibiny Massif, Murmansk Oblast, Russia, is a major apatite deposit operated by Apatite Joint-Stock Company, a leading producer of phosphate raw materials for fertiliser. Located near Lake Umbozero, the deposit lies within an alkaline massif featuring ultra-agpaitic pegmatites, urtite, foidolites, apatite-nepheline rocks, and various mineral veins. Discovered in 1959, Koashva hosts a series of lens-shaped ore bodies extending over three kilometres, with thickness decreasing from 200–300 metres near the surface to several metres at depth, making it the richest deposit in the Khibiny and a key resource for phosphate production.

The Buckwheat Pit at the former Franklin Mine in Sussex County, New Jersey, represents a remnant of historic zinc ore extraction. Opened in 1866 in a buckwheat field and later expanded as the Taylor Mine, it operated with an aerial tramway until its closure around 1912, while underground mining nearby persisted until 1954. The site contains zinc and iron ore veins within Franklin Marble, a late Ordovician lamprophyre dike, and vuggy Buckwheat Dolomite Marble of Paleozoic age. These distinctive formations produced an extraordinary range of minerals, including more than one hundred species known to fluoresce under ultraviolet light.

The Korshunovskoye iron deposit near Zheleznogorsk-Ilimsky in the Nizhneilimsky District of Irkutsk Oblast, Russia, is a notable skarn-type formation. It contains iron-rich mineralization associated with explosive pipes hosted in salt-bearing strata. This geological setting has produced significant concentrations of iron alongside boron-related minerals, reflecting complex processes of metasomatism and hydrothermal activity.

The Udachnaya mine is one of the world’s largest and most important diamond deposits, located in the Daldyn area of the Mirninsky District in the Sakha Republic (Yakutia), Russia. Discovered in 1955 shortly after the famous Mir pipe, it gave rise to the nearby town of Udachny and became a cornerstone of Russia’s diamond industry under the management of the state-controlled company Alrosa. Geologically, Udachnaya consists of two intersecting kimberlite pipes intruded into Lower Ordovician limestone and forming part of the Daldyn–Alakit kimberlite field. The deposit is renowned for its exceptional scientific value. It contains abundant mantle-derived xenoliths, including eclogite, dunite, and peridotite, providing rare insight into Earth’s deep interior. The Udachnaya-East pipe is particularly unusual because its deep kimberlite is almost completely free of serpentinization, meaning primary minerals such as olivine are exceptionally well preserved. The mine is also a type locality for several rare minerals and is notable for unusual accumulations of alkali carbonates, chlorides, and minerals such as djerfisherite and zemkorite. The open pit reached a depth of about 640 meters, making it one of the deepest open-pit diamond mines ever excavated, before surface mining ended in 2015. Production has since continued underground in extreme Arctic conditions, with winter temperatures frequently dropping below minus fifty degrees Celsius. Recent investments approved in 2025 aim to extend underground mining until at least 2055. Despite environmental and logistical challenges, Udachnaya remains a major contributor to global diamond supply, accounting for more than ten percent of Russia’s rough diamond output.

Site: Ba-Phalaborwa Local Municipality, Mopani District, Limpopo
metadata: Palabora Mine in Phalaborwa, Limpopo, is operated by Palabora Mining Company Limited, established in 1956. It is South Africa’s only active copper producer and the largest employer in Ba-Phalaborwa. Initially an open-pit, it transitioned to underground mining between 2001 and 2004. The site yields copper cathode and rod, as well as nickel sulfate, sulfuric acid, magnetite, vermiculite, precious metals, uranium, zirconium, platinum, and phosphate. The mine lies within the 1,950-hectare Phalaborwa Complex of pyroxenite, foskorite, and sövite carbonatite, containing vast apatite resources and accessory minerals including uraninite, thorianite, and baddeleyite extracted through large-scale operations.

The Argyle Diamond Mine in the remote East Kimberley region of Western Australia was discovered in 1979 and became the world’s first commercially successful diamond operation exploiting a lamproite pipe. Commencing production in 1983 and transitioning from open-pit to underground mining in 2013, it yielded more than 865 million carats of rough diamonds before ceasing operations in November 2020. Operated by Rio Tinto, Argyle was the largest global producer of natural diamonds by volume and the principal source of pink and red stones, also supplying rare blue, champagne, cognac, near-gem and industrial diamonds over its 37-year lifespan.

The San Valentín Mine near La Unión in Murcia, Spain, is a large open-pit operation historically exploited for lead, zinc, and iron ores. Extensive mining and processing of pyrite, sphalerite, galena, and cassiterite from nearby deposits between 1952 and 1981 generated substantial flotation tailings and waste materials. These abandoned deposits, including the Descargador mine pond, have altered the local landscape, disrupted natural drainage, and contributed to widespread soil, water, and ecosystem contamination. The resulting geochemical hazards pose ongoing risks to human health and threaten the Mar Menor coastal lagoon, highlighting the urgent need for environmental remediation.

Site: Moab-Setting Lakes Area, Thompson Nickel Belt, Manitoba
metadata: The Thompson Mine in the Moab-Setting Lakes area of the Thompson Nickel Belt in Manitoba, Canada, is an open-pit operation that began in 1986 to extract high-grade nickel sulfide. It is located near the planned city of Thompson, established in 1956 along the Burntwood River, which serves as the largest urban and service hub in northern Manitoba, providing health care, education, retail trade, and transportation links to many remote Indigenous communities. The region, the world’s fifth-largest nickel belt, has yielded billions of pounds of nickel yet remains underexplored, supported by extensive infrastructure and clean hydroelectric power for future low-carbon development.

The Mary Kathleen Mine in north-western Queensland, Australia, operated east of Mount Isa between 1956 and 1963 and again from 1975 to 1982. Developed by Mary Kathleen Uranium Limited, it produced almost 9,000 tonnes of uranium oxide to supply overseas markets, alongside large reserves of rare earth oxides. An architect-designed township supported about 1,000 residents before both mine and town were dismantled after closure in 1984, marking Australia’s largest auction. The site hosted the country’s first large-scale open-cut rehabilitation program to cover radioactive waste and promote revegetation, although signs of deterioration and leaching are now evident at the former operation.

Site: Sepon Mine, Vilabouly District, Savannakhet Province
metadata: The Khanong Open Pit at the Sepon Mine in the Vilabouly District of Savannakhet Province, Laos, opened in 2003 and comprises three copper pits and eighteen gold pits, most of which are now depleted. Initially focused on extensive gold production, operations shifted to high-grade copper averaging 5.4 percent, with additional pits mined until 2020 before copper output was reduced and gold processing resumed. Owned and operated by MMG, a subsidiary of China Minmetals, the mine is renowned for producing rare azurite and malachite specimens, making it one of the most distinctive mineral locations in south-central Laos.

Site: Tharsis Mines, Tharsis, Alosno, Huelva, Andalusia
metadata: The Filón Sur open pit at the Tharsis Mines in Alosno, Huelva, Andalusia, Spain, forms part of the Tharsis-La Zarza mining basin within the Iberian Pyrite Belt. Exploited since antiquity, the area reached peak production in the 19th and 20th centuries under the British Tharsis Sulphur and Copper Company, which introduced modern extraction techniques and surface mining through the cortas system. The operation supported industrial facilities, railways, and mining towns, establishing a significant mining-industrial complex. Today, the site preserves extensive historical and industrial heritage, with ongoing initiatives promoting conservation and tourism to showcase its legacy within Spain’s mining history.

The Reocín Mine in Cantabria, Spain, is the country’s largest stratabound carbonate-hosted zinc-lead deposit. Located on the Santillana Synclinal border in the Vasco Cantabria basin, about 30 km southeast of Santander, it was closed in 2003. Open-pit operations created a large excavation that gradually filled with water from the Saja River, rain, and minor diapir inflows, forming a controlled mining lake. Water infiltration, averaging 1,100 to 1,299 liters per second, posed significant operational and stability challenges, requiring careful drainage management based on the site’s unique geological and hydrogeological conditions.

The Lavender Pit in Bisbee, Cochise County, Arizona, was an open-pit copper mine operated by Phelps Dodge from 1950 to 1974, producing approximately 600,000 tons of copper from 86 million tons of ore, with gold, silver, and turquoise as by-products. Named after Harrison M. Lavender, the pit replaced the earlier Sacramento Hill mine and features steep sides due to competent host rock. Covering 300 acres and reaching 274 meters deep, it generated 256 million tons of waste, some acid-leached for additional copper. The adjacent Cochise deposit, north of the pit, holds potential for future mining.

Site: Climax Mining District, Lake County, Colorado
metadata: The Climax Mine in Lake County, Colorado, is a major molybdenum operation covering approximately 14,350 acres. Established in 1915, it once supplied three quarters of the world’s molybdenum. After a 17-year closure, the mine reopened in 2012 as a surface open pit with a 25,000-ton-per-day mill capable of producing about 30 million pounds annually. The deposit features multiple shells of molybdenite-bearing stockwork veins within a siliceous rhyolite porphyry stock. The mill also recovers pyrite, wolframite, cassiterite, and monazite. The mine is owned by Climax Molybdenum Company, a subsidiary of Freeport-McMoRan.

Site: Kramer Borate Deposit, Boron, Kern County, California
metadata: The Rio Tinto Borax Mine in Boron, California, is the largest open-pit borax operation in the world, producing nearly half of global borates. Discovered in 1913, mining began with shafts in the 1920s, and the current open-pit operation opened in 1957 under U.S. Borax, now part of Rio Tinto Group. Reserves are sufficient for economic production through the early 2040s. The mine is operated by Rio Tinto’s Borax division. In 2019, a pilot project was launched to recover lithium from mining waste, expanding the site’s contribution to mineral production beyond borates.

The La Parrilla Mine, also known as the Adelaida open pit, is situated in Almoharín, Cáceres, Extremadura, Spain and is the country’s largest tungsten deposit. Currently inactive, it holds substantial reserves estimated at around 30 million tonnes of ore grading 0.11 percent tungsten. The deposit consists of a quartz vein stockwork within metapelitic rocks and contains diverse sulphide minerals including galena, sphalerite, and native silver. With direct highway access and proximity to Atlantic and Mediterranean ports, the site is part of Spain’s strategic projects under the European Union Critical Raw Materials Act, which seeks to meet the Union’s demand for raw materials by 2030.

Site: Girilambone, Canbelego County, New South Wales
metadata: The Girilambone Copper Mine, including the Murrawombie open pit and West Bogan Mine, in central New South Wales. Copper was discovered in 1875. The site was initially worked with limited success due to metallurgical challenges and inadequate processing technology. Redevelopment introduced gravity concentration and chemical leaching, but large-scale extraction began in the 1990s using innovative heap leaching, solvent extraction, and electrowinning, producing about 400 tonnes of copper annually. The site holds cultural significance for the Weilwan people, with “Girilambone” meaning “place of falling stars” .

The Ouenza Mine in Tébessa Province, Algeria, is a large open-pit operation extracting iron carbonate deposits from Aptian neritic limestones near Triassic evaporitic diapirs. Producing between 2 and 2.5 million tons of iron ore annually, it contains reserves estimated at 120 to 150 million tons. The ore comprises siderite, hematite, and goethite, which influence its iron content and quality. Together with the Boukhadra Mine, Ouenza was managed by Société des Mines de Fer de l’Est, owned mainly by Manal Group and partly by EPE SIDER El Hadjar S.p.A., highlighting its importance in Algeria’s iron production.

Site: Antofagasta de la Sierra Department, Catamarca Province
metadata: The Tincalayu Mine, situated in the Tincalayu borax deposit at the northern edge of the Salar del Hombre Muerto in Catamarca Province, Argentina, is a major borate producer in South America. It operates two open pits, the largest measuring 1,500 meters wide, 500 meters long, and 100 meters deep, at an altitude of about 4,000 meters above sea level. Formerly exploited by Boroquímica and linked to processing at Campo Quijano, the mine lies within a high-altitude salt pan that also forms part of the Lithium Triangle, a globally significant source of lithium for renewable energy and electric vehicle technologies.

Site: Pirquitas Mine, Mina Pirquitas, Rinconada Department, Jujuy Province
metadata: The Oploca Vein System, on the southern margin of the Pirquitas Mine in Jujuy Province, Argentina, trends east-west and is shifting from underground extraction at the San Miguel shaft to early-stage open-pit operations. The Pirquitas Mine applies conventional drilling, blasting, and grade control techniques to mine approximately 50,000 tonnes per day, feeding a processing plant with three crushing stages capable of 6,000 tonnes per day. Ore is upgraded through a preconcentration jig system. As of September 30, 2011, proven and probable reserves exceeded 15 million tonnes containing substantial silver, tin, and zinc, highlighting the site’s significant polymetallic resources.

Site: Veladero Mine, Tudcum, Iglesia Department, San Juan Province
metadata: The Amable Pit is part of the Veladero Mine in the Frontera District of San Juan Province, Argentina, situated at elevations between 4,000 and 4,850 meters above sea level and approximately 374 kilometres northwest of San Juan city. Operated as a 50/50 joint venture with Shandong Gold, Veladero is a large open-pit truck and shovel operation producing gold and silver through heap leaching since 2005 and was the first in the country to meet the Towards Sustainable Mining Standard. Current plans focus on extending operations beyond 2034, supported by intensified exploration of high-sulfidation targets within the district.

Cadia Hill Mine, part of the Cadia Valley operations near Orange in New South Wales, Australia, comprises extensive open-cut and underground gold and copper mines operated by Newmont. Developed since the 1990s, it is the country’s second largest open-cut mine after the Super Pit at Kalgoorlie and a major regional employer with a projected life of several decades. Adjacent Ridgeway and Cadia East underground mines add substantial resources. The operation is a pioneer in block cave mining and uses advanced automation, including remotely orchestrated loaders and auxiliary equipment, to enhance productivity and efficiency.

Bengalla Open Cut Mine near Muswellbrook in New South Wales is a large black coal operation with approval to extract up to 15 million tonnes of run-of-mine coal annually until 2039. Employing over 600 people and operating continuously, it is recognised as a cost-competitive producer within the lowest quartile of global seaborne thermal coal costs. The mining fleet supports a 13.4 million tonne growth project, with the coal handling and preparation plant ramping up after a tailings capacity upgrade. Current efforts focus on extending the mine’s life beyond 2037 and completing new infrastructure.

Mangoola Open Cut Mine in New South Wales is a black coal operation originally developed by Centennial Coal to supply fuel for electricity generation. Approved in 2006 amid environmental debate, it became a landmark case when the Land and Environment Court ruled the government had inadequately assessed greenhouse gas emissions. Centennial later sold the project to Xstrata, which was subsequently acquired by Glencore. Production began in 2011, and in 2021 the mine secured approval to continue operating until 2030, reflecting its ongoing role in regional energy supply and the evolving regulatory landscape for coal mining in Australia.

Mount Pleasant Open Cut Mine near Muswellbrook in New South Wales is a greenfield black coal operation extending over six square kilometres and reaching about 220 metres in depth. Managed by Thiess Mining, it holds an estimated 474 million tonnes of reserves producing high-quality, low-impurity coal for international markets. The operation uses controlled blasting to fragment rock before excavation and rail transport. Recently, the New South Wales Court of Appeal overturned approval for an expansion of this project, finding that the Independent Planning Commission had not adequately assessed the climate impacts of emissions from the combustion of exported coal.

The Murrawombie Open Pit, historically known as the Girilambone Copper Mine in central western New South Wales, lies two kilometres west of Girilambone and was first discovered in 1875. Modern mining began in 1992 under the Girilambone Copper Company, a joint venture later wholly owned by Straits Resources, and produced copper oxide ore through heap leaching, solvent extraction, and electrowinning until 2002. The pit reached about 150 metres in depth within highly foliated meta-sediments hosting a massive sulphide deposit. Residual leaching continues today, recovering around 400 tonnes of copper annually from the remaining heap leach solution as copper cement.

Gibsons Open Cut, is a gold mine located approximately 1.25 kilometres south of Halls Peak in Clarke County, New South Wales. Historically, the mine operated through a combination of shafts, adits, and open-cut methods. A November 2024 pre-feasibility study confirmed the Mt Gibson Gold Project as a large-scale operation, projecting annual gold production exceeding 150,000 ounces during the first fifteen years, highlighting its significant potential as a long-term contributor to regional gold output.

Glendell Open Cut Mine in Durham County, New South Wales, was part of the Mt. Owen/Glendell Operation, producing thermal and semi-soft metallurgical coal from seams 0.3 to 3.5 metres thick. Operations ceased in November 2022, while Glencore has submitted a modification application to extend mining north of the Glendell Pit, targeting the Hebden Seam. The proposed Glendell Pit Extension aims to continue coal production, maintaining the site’s role in supplying both thermal and metallurgical coal to domestic and international markets.

Liddell Open Cut Mine, located 25 kilometres northwest of Singleton in the Upper Hunter Valley, New South Wales, was a century-old operation producing thermal and metallurgical coal for export. Mining concluded in 2023, initiating the Liddell Coal Closure Project focused on site rehabilitation. A  May 2023 modification to development consent permits ongoing use of the mine’s final voids until 2050 for tailings and water storage. This transition reflects a shift from active extraction to environmental management, ensuring safe closure and continued utility of the site’s remaining infrastructure.

Site: Muswellbrook, Durham County, New South Wales
metadata: Mt Arthur North Mine, part of the Mount Arthur Coal Complex near Muswellbrook, New South Wales, is an open-cut operation opened by the Broken Hill Proprietary Company in 2002 and the state’s largest coal mine. It has a capacity of 20 million tonnes per year, mining 21 distinct seams and employing 2,000 staff. Coal is transported via Aurizon to the Port of Newcastle for export. Following a 2020 shift to export-only production, BHP considered selling the mine but retained it in 2022, planning closure by 2030. Post-mining, the site may be repurposed for a pumped-storage hydroelectricity project.

Site: Muswellbrook, Durham County, New South Wales
metadata: Muswellbrook Open Cut Mine in Durham County, New South Wales, concluded over a century of coal production in 2022, with final coal transport completed in March 2023. Established by the Muswellbrook Coal Company, the site began underground mining in 1907 and open-cut operations in 1944. Rehabilitation is now underway under Idemitsu Australia, which is implementing productive post-mining land uses, including a pumped-storage hydropower project with AGL and a large-scale solar farm with ESCO Pacific, demonstrating a transition from active mining to sustainable energy-focused land management

Ravensworth Open Cut Mine, fully owned by Glencore, is located in the Hunter Valley between Muswellbrook and Singleton, New South Wales. The operation produces thermal and metallurgical coal for export using a fleet of hydraulic shovels, excavators, and rear dump trucks, extracting approximately 13.3 million tonnes of run-of-mine coal and 63 million cubic metres of waste annually. In 2024, Ravensworth employed 760 people and rehabilitated 55 hectares. Ravensworth Underground ceased operations in 2014, entering care and maintenance, and was officially sealed in 2023 with surface infrastructure decommissioned, marking a complete transition to open-cut mining.

Spruce Pine Quarry, located in Bakersville within North Carolina’s Spruce Pine Mining District, lies in the North Toe River valley of the Blue Ridge Mountains. The district produces mica, kaolin, feldspar, and the world’s largest concentration of high-purity quartz, supplying 70–90 percent of global demand for semiconductor and solar photovoltaic manufacturing. Ground mica is recovered during mineral extraction, while sheet mica remains unmined. As the hub of major mining activity, Spruce Pine is considered a critical point in the global supply chain, with analysts warning that disruption could halt integrated circuit production worldwide within six months.

Rix’s Creek Open Cut Mine in Durham County, New South Wales, is part of the Integra Coal operation, 61.2 percent owned by Vale Australia with partners Posco and Toyota. Originally slated to close in 2020, the mine received approval in 2019 to operate until 2040 following multiple extensions. The New South Wales Environment Protection Authority has fined the mine for saline groundwater discharges from historical underground workings, which can disrupt aquatic ecosystems. Management measures, including borehole capping and pump reinstatement, have been implemented. In June 2025, a discharge occurred from an uncapped borehole drilled to assess old workings and improve seepage control.

Duralie Coal Mine, also known as Stratford Duralie, is an open-cut operation in Gloucester County, New South Wales, with coal reserves of 322.3 million tonnes of coking coal, ranking among the largest in Asia and globally. The mine has an annual production capacity of five million tonnes. Coal extraction is scheduled to conclude in 2024, after which closure activities will include decommissioning infrastructure and rehabilitating the site to an approved final landform. Environmental monitoring and maintenance of rehabilitated areas are expected to continue for 10 to 15 years, with the New South Wales Government required to assess and approve closure works before mining leases can be relinquished

Avon North Open Pit, part of the Stratford Mining Complex in the Gloucester Basin, New South Wales, is associated with the Stratford, Duralie, and Grant & Chainey deposits. While Stratford Mine ceased coal production in late 2021, Avon North may still have limited related activity. Stratford transitioned to closure in 2024, with bulk earthworks, reshaping, and topsoiling underway, and demolition of the coal handling and preparation plant commencing in May 2025 to safely remove hazardous materials, dismantle equipment, and dispose of foundations. Coal extraction at Duralie ended in December 2021, followed by rehandling, decommissioning, site rehabilitation, and ongoing environmental monitoring.

The United Wambo Open Cut Mine in the Hunter Valley, New South Wales, began production in 2020 as a 50:50 joint venture between United Collieries (Glencore and MEU) and Wambo Coal (Peabody). It integrates the former Wambo and United open-cut operations to produce thermal and semi-soft metallurgical coal for export. The mine has estimated reserves of 176 million tonnes, an annual production capacity of 10 million tonnes, an expected life of 23 years, and represents an investment of about $380 million. This development proceeds despite official forecasts of Australian thermal coal exports consistently overestimating demand and lacking scenarios that reflect urgent global commitments to reduce coal use.

Collier Open Cut, located near Condobolin in the Mineral Hill field of central New South Wales, Australia, was a major copper mining operation active during the early to mid-twentieth century. Initially developed as an open pit, it later transitioned to underground mining, reflecting evolving practices within the industry. The site formed part of a historically important district renowned for its rich and varied mineral deposits. Collier contributed significantly to the region’s mining output and development, with renewed activity and further development occurring in the 1940s and 1950s.

Site: Northparkes Mine Project, Goonumbla, Kennedy County, New South Wales
metadata: Northparkes Mine near Goonumbla in central-west New South Wales is Australia’s second largest porphyry copper deposit after Cadia, with an endowment of about 5.5 million ounces of gold and 4.5 million tons of copper. Operated as a joint venture between Rio Tinto and Sumitomo Group, it produces high-grade copper-gold concentrate for international smelters. Exploration began in the early 1970s, leading to the discovery of multiple ore bodies, including Endeavour22. Northparkes pioneered block-cave underground mining in Australia in 1997 and currently operates two open pits and one underground mine within a belt of Ordovician volcanic rocks.

Site: Captain's Flat, Lake George, Murray County, New South Wales
metadata: Elliots No. 1 Open Cut at Captain’s Flat in the Lachlan Fold Belt of south-eastern New South Wales formed part of a major volcanic-hosted massive sulphide zinc-lead-copper deposit 50 kilometres south-east of Canberra. The ore comprised pyrite, sphalerite, galena, chalcopyrite, tennantite, arsenopyrite, gold and rare pyrrhotite with gangue of quartz, chert, carbonates, chlorite and sericite. Mining began after copper was discovered in 1874 and evolved through several companies and smelting operations. From 1882 to 1962 about 4 million tonnes of ore were extracted, yielding high grades of zinc, lead, copper, silver and gold. Prolonged miner strikes in 1948-49 and 1954-55 disrupted production before the mine finally closed in 1962.

Bloomfield Mine and Colliery is an open-cut coal operation in New South Wales, Australia, with a history stretching back to 1937. Underground mining ceased in 1992, while open-cut extraction, first introduced in 1966, now defines the site’s operations. The mine produces around 1.3 million tonnes of coal per year, primarily high-quality thermal coal with some semi-soft coking coal, sourced from a multi-seam, multi-bench system that works across up to 13 coal seams using large-scale earthmoving equipment. Run-of-mine coal is delivered to the onsite coal handling and preparation plant, enabling both in-house washing and bulk handling services for partner producers targeting the Asian export market. Covering 299 hectares and supported by proved and probable reserves of approximately 8 million tonnes, the mine employs about 100 people. Bloomfield Mine forms part of the Bloomfield Group’s broader operations, which also include Rix’s Creek Mine, with coal from both mines railed to the Port of Newcastle for loading via Port Waratah Coal Services. In 2025, the group faced industrial and regulatory challenges, including a rejected enterprise agreement at Rix’s Creek—where 92.6% of workers voted no—and a $30,000 fine for a saline water discharge incident. Despite these issues, the future of Bloomfield Mine was strengthened when the Minister for Planning approved an extension of its operational life until 2030, ensuring its continued contribution to regional employment and coal exports.

Bulga Open Cut Coal Mine, located about 15 kilometres southwest of Singleton near the villages of Broke and Bulga in New South Wales’ Upper Hunter Valley, forms the core of the Bulga coal complex operated by Bulga Coal Management, a Glencore subsidiary. Mining first began in 1982 and expanded into underground operations in 1992. Over more than 40 years, the complex has produced high-grade thermal and semi-soft coking coal. Today, the open-cut operation covers roughly 5,500 hectares and forms part of a joint venture in which Saxonvale Coal Pty Ltd holds 87.5% and Nippon Steel Australia Pty Ltd holds 12.5%. Bulga Coal employs around 750 people and continues to supply Asian markets, exporting approximately 12 million tonnes of semi-soft coking and thermal coal annually via rail to the Port of Newcastle. The site also hosts a Coal Handling and Preparation Plant and maintains substantial mineable reserves—159 million tonnes as of 2022—within a total resource base of 2.83 billion tonnes. Bulga has an established rehabilitation program, with more than 1,000 hectares restored by December 2024. While operations are approved through 2035, a methane plume detected on January 21, 2025, displayed an estimated emission rate of about 2,600 kg per hour, underscoring the ongoing importance of rigorous emissions oversight.

Mount Thorley Warkworth Coal Mine is an integrated coal mining operation in the Hunter Valley of New South Wales, located about 15 kilometres southwest of Singleton. Operating 24 hours a day, the site combines the adjacent Mount Thorley and Warkworth open-cut mines, which began coal extraction in 1981 and were formally merged in 2004. The operation is run by Coal & Allied Proprietary Limited, a wholly owned subsidiary of Yancoal Australia, which also holds majority ownership stakes in both mines. The mine produces semi-soft coking and thermal coal for domestic and export markets and is approved to continue mining until February 2037. Open-cut capacity is about 12 million tonnes per annum, with an additional 6 million tonnes per annum anticipated from a proposed underground expansion currently under assessment. Historically, the site has been permitted to extract up to 28 million tonnes of run-of-mine coal annually, though production in 2021 totalled approximately 11.2 million tonnes. The mine contains significant long-term resources, including 613 million tonnes identified in 2015 and 315 million tonnes of mineable reserves as of 2018. Environmental compliance has been an ongoing issue: in 2019, regulators issued notices citing delays and documentation gaps in rehabilitation efforts, and in 2020 the mine owners received a 15,000-dollar fine after a blasting-related dust cloud affected nearby properties. More recently, on 13 February 2025, a methane plume with an estimated emission rate of roughly 1,560 kilograms per hour was detected over the open-cut mine.

Site: Moolarben Coal Complex, Phillip County, New South Wales
metadata: The Moolarben Coal Mine, also known as the Moolarben Coal Complex, is a major surface and underground coal operation located about 40 kilometres east of Mudgee in the Upper Hunter Valley of New South Wales. Operated by Moolarben Coal Operations Proprietary Limited, a subsidiary of Yancoal Australia, the mine is jointly owned by Yancoal, which holds a ninety-five percent share, and Kores Australia Moolarben Coal Proprietary Limited, which holds the remaining five percent. Coal production began with the first open-cut pit in 2010, followed by the start of underground longwall mining in 2012. Early operations produced roughly eight million tonnes of thermal coal from the open-cut mine and four million tonnes from the underground mine, supported by a project investment of approximately four hundred and five million dollars, including construction of a coal preparation plant. Over time, the joint venture structure evolved, with Yancoal describing Moolarben as an unincorporated venture in which it owns eighty percent, alongside partners including Sojitz Corporation of Japan and a consortium led by Korea Resources Corporation, Korea Electric Power Company, several Korean generator subsidiaries, and Hanwha Corporation Limited. The resource base is substantial, with an estimated 1.03 billion tonnes of thermal coal as of 2022 and mineable reserves of 194 million tonnes. Production reached 16.9 million tonnes per annum in 2022, and the complex is planned to expand to four open-cut mines and three underground longwall mines. A 2019 approval allows boundary extensions and additional output of three million tonnes of run-of-mine coal annually, enabling up to twenty million tonnes of run-of-mine coal per year from open-cut operations through 2038.

Site: Moolarben Coal Complex, Phillip County, New South Wales
metadata: The Moolarben Coal Mine, also known as the Moolarben Coal Complex, is a major surface and underground coal operation located about 40 kilometres east of Mudgee in the Upper Hunter Valley of New South Wales. The Wilpinjong Coal Mine is a major open-cut thermal coal operation located in Wollar, New South Wales, about forty kilometres north-east of Mudgee and ten kilometres south-east of the Ulan mining district. Commissioned in 2006 and covering roughly 2,800 hectares, the mine is owned by Peabody Energy, which acquired it from Excel Coal, and operated by Thiess under contract. Wilpinjong extracts coal from the fifteen-metre-thick Ulan Seam within the Permian-aged Illawarra Coal Measures, a deposit composed of ten plies ranging from high-quality coal to stony and high-ash material. Its shallow geological setting makes underground mining uneconomical, and waste rock is placed in adjacent voids or temporary emplacements before being used for backfilling. Since opening, Wilpinjong has undergone several expansions, including a ninety-million-dollar project completed in 2013 that raised annual output by up to three million tonnes. Today, production exceeds twelve million tonnes per year, with long-term domestic supply contracts to Macquarie Generation’s Bayswater and Liddell power stations and additional exports to Asian customers via the Port of Newcastle. The on-site preparation plant washes about thirty percent of raw coal, producing domestic and export thermal coal with a specific energy of approximately 6,750 kilocalories per kilogram. Wilpinjong has been a significant economic presence in the Mudgee region for nearly two decades, supporting more than seven hundred jobs and ninety-six million dollars in wages. However, Peabody Energy’s proposed life-of-mine expansion—which would add eight hundred hectares and raise capacity from fifteen to sixteen million tonnes per year—has drawn strong opposition from Wollar residents, who argue the mine’s growth threatens the village’s viability, risks displacement and contributes to the loss of essential services such as the community’s general store.

Site: Broken Hill District, Yancowinna County, New South Wales
metadata: Block Fourteen is one of the historic leases within the Broken Hill mining district of far western New South Wales, a region built upon what became the world’s richest silver–lead–zinc orebody. First worked by the Broken Hill Proprietary Block Fourteen Company Limited until 1930, the lease later passed to North Broken Hill Limited in 1942 and then to Broken Hill South Limited in 1962. The site developed into a large open-cut pit noted for outcrops of rhodonite in galena, and over time it absorbed the former British Mine on Blocks Fifteen and Sixteen as well as parts of the adjacent Proprietary Mine. Block Fourteen sits beside the South Mine, established in 1885 as the Broken Hill South Silver Mining Company Limited, which originally worked Blocks Seven and Eight before taking over Blocks Nine through Sixteen after the departure of the Broken Hill Proprietary Company in the 1940s. These blocks included the Central Mine on Block Nine, a standalone operation on Block Ten, the Proprietary Mine on Blocks Eleven to Thirteen, and Block Fourteen as an individual mine. Broken Hill itself grew into a prosperous mining centre and is recognised today as Australia’s longest-running mining town, listed on the National Heritage List in 2015. Yet the legacy of mineral extraction has carried substantial health costs: lead contamination remains one of the city’s most serious public-health issues, with mandatory infant blood-lead testing and several neighbourhoods near the main mine—such as Gaffney Street, Eyre Street and Slag Street—identified as among the most contaminated residential areas in New South Wales. Alongside its mining heritage, Broken Hill is notable for its deep trade-union history and its pivotal role in shaping Australia’s labour movement.

Site: Broken Hill District, Yancowinna County, New South Wales
metadata: The Kintore Open Pit is a notable component of the Broken Hill mining district, forming part of the larger Rasp Mine gold complex in far western New South Wales. Named after the historic Kintore shaft of the Central Mine (Block Nine), the open-cut operation was active from the late 1980s until 1996, primarily targeting the remaining oxide portion of the southern end of the Broken Hill Lode. Operated alongside the nearby Block Fourteen Open Pit by Normandy Mining, which was later acquired by Newmont Mining, the site produced significant quantities of gold, generating tens of thousands of ounces in its initial years, including from deposits such as Kintore West. Beyond ore extraction, the pit played a critical role in waste rock and tailings management, particularly for lead-contaminated material, with co-disposal strategies creating rehabilitation caps. The pit also provides underground access through portals, linking surface operations to deeper mining phases. Geotechnical safety was a key focus, with slope stability analyses conducted on pit walls to ensure operational integrity. As part of the broader Goldfields region, Kintore Open Pit is integrated with other nearby operations, including the Broken Hill Proprietary Pit, contributing to the overall productivity and infrastructure of the Rasp Mine. Today, it stands as an important transitional site, illustrating the evolution from primary gold production to a multifunctional facility supporting waste management, rehabilitation, and underground mining access, reflecting the complexity and historical significance of mining in the Broken Hill district.

Site: Broken Hill District, Yancowinna County, New South Wales
metadata: The Blackwood Open Cut was a key phase in the historic Broken Hill South Mine in Yancowinna County, New South Wales, Australia, operated by Minerals Mining & Metallurgy from the early 1970s to 1990. Following the cessation of deep underground mining at Broken Hill South Limited in 1972, the Blackwood Open Cut focused on extracting lead, zinc, and silver from the upper, gossanous (oxidized) zones of the main lode, effectively working over the remnants of previous underground operations. The open-cut method allowed Minerals Mining & Metallurgy to access lower-grade, weathered ore that had previously been uneconomical to mine underground. The Blackwood pit, established around 1974 on the old British Mine’s Block Fifteen, was later complemented by the Kintore Open Cut on Block Ten in 1983. In 1988, Pasminco, formed from the merger of Consolidated Zinc and other Australian zinc processing companies, acquired North Broken Hill’s assets, including these operations. The Blackwood Open Cut marked a broader transition in the Broken Hill mining district from traditional deep-level extraction to surface-focused mining techniques, sustaining production from one of the world’s richest lead-zinc-silver deposits. Over its operational history, Broken Hill’s mines have claimed more than 800 lives, commemorated by the Line of Lode Miner’s Memorial overlooking the city. Blackwood Open Cut’s contribution to the Broken Hill orebody underscores its importance in both the region’s industrial heritage and ongoing legacy of mineral extraction, reflecting the evolution of mining practices in one of Australia’s most historically significant mining centers.

Site: Hilton District, City of Mount Isa, Queensland
metadata: The Handlebar Hill Open Cut is part of the historic mining landscape of Mount Isa, Queensland, Australia, operated by Xstrata Zinc as part of Glencore’s Mount Isa Mines. Mining of lead, zinc, and silver began in October 2007 and continued until 2014, after which the mine was placed under care and maintenance due to low metal prices. The Handlebar Hill and Magazine waste rock facilities, which received approximately 76 million tonnes of waste during operations, have since been rehabilitated, with reshaping and capping of 230 hectares of land. Mount Isa itself has long been a centre of mineral extraction and smelting, ranking among the top two copper mining and smelting operations in Australia. Copper and lead are smelted on site, with copper anodes and zinc concentrate transported 900 kilometres to the city and port of Townsville, and lead ingots shipped to Britain for silver extraction. The lead smelter stack, built in 1978, rises 270 metres and remains a prominent landmark visible across the city and up to 40 kilometres away. Mount Isa sits on the traditional lands of the Kalkadoon people, whose language, Kalkatunga, was spoken widely in the area. The region has a long pre-European history, with evidence of stone tool production dating back 20,000 years. European contact in the mid-1860s led to conflict as settlers encroached on Kalkadoon lands, culminating in the Kalkadoon Wars from 1871 to 1884, one of Australia’s most notable Indigenous resistances. The Handlebar Hill Open Cut exemplifies both the industrial and cultural history of Mount Isa, reflecting centuries of human activity.

Site: Mount Isa Mines, Mount Isa, City of Mount Isa, Queensland
metadata: The Black Rock Open Cut was a major Mount Isa operation that extracted copper oxide ore—along with associated lead and zinc minerals—from near-surface sections of the ore body until wall instability forced its closure in 1965. Its development formed part of a broader transformation at Mount Isa during the mid-twentieth century. Between 1952 and 1960, exploration significantly expanded copper reserves, prompting Mount Isa Mines to embark on a large-scale production increase. As new deposits were confirmed, the company built the Townsville Copper Refinery, an electrolytic facility producing copper cathode for domestic and international customers. In 1959, Mount Isa erected its now-iconic candy-striped Copper Smelter stack, a landmark that took eight months to complete and symbolised the rapid growth of the region’s copper industry. More than fifty years after the Black Rock Open Cut closed, the area again became central to Mount Isa’s mining evolution with the launch of the Black Rock Cave project in 2018. This initiative replaced sub-level open stoping with sub-level caving, allowing Glencore to extract ore located within and beneath the former open cut. Production from Black Rock Cave began between 2020 and 2024, with the underground ore body expected to yield roughly 1.8 million tonnes of copper ore. With copper extraction now ending, Glencore is redirecting investment toward the region’s zinc and lead assets, particularly the Black Star Open Cut—located over the same area as the underground mine—which could provide up to 400 jobs and operate for as long as twenty years.

The Mary Kathleen uranium mine, located east of Mount Isa, was one of Australia’s earliest and most notable uranium operations, developed after a major deposit was discovered in 1954. Operated by Mary Kathleen Uranium Limited, the project began mining in late 1956 and commissioned its treatment plant in 1958. The mine and the purpose-built township functioned in two distinct phases—1956 to 1963 and again from 1975 to 1982—ultimately producing nearly 9,000 tonnes of uranium oxide, commonly known as yellowcake. Initially established to supply the United Kingdom Atomic Energy Authority, the mine later provided uranium to Japan, Germany, and the United States. The open-cut pit, said to be about 250 metres deep and four kilometres wide at the top, originally contained around 10,000 tonnes of uranium oxide and 200,000 tonnes of rare earth oxides. By the 1980s, Mary Kathleen had become a focal point of union and environmental opposition, centred on the risks of radioactive leakage, government subsidies, and the end use of exported uranium—concerns heightened when two tonnes of yellowcake from the site were discovered in Sydney. When production ceased in 1982, the mine, mill, and town were dismantled and auctioned in what was described as Australia’s largest industrial sale. Rehabilitation, completed by 1985, was the country’s first major open-cut uranium mine restoration, covering radioactive tailings and attempting to return the landscape to native vegetation. Despite this early effort, the site has since deteriorated, with evidence of leaching from the pit walls and tailings, and today remains a ghost town.

Site: Kapunda, Light Regional Council, South Australia
metadata: The Charlotte Open Cut forms part of the historic Kapunda Copper Mine in South Australia, the nation’s first successful metal mine and a major driver of early colonial industry. Copper was discovered at Kapunda in 1842 by Francis Dutton, and by 1844 underground mining was underway, with ore hauled by bullock dray to Port Adelaide—a six-day journey—before being shipped to Swansea in Wales for smelting. By 1850 the mine was producing around 100 tonnes of ore each month; German-made smelters arriving in 1849 allowed processing to shift to South Australia, though ships continued to return from England with coal to fuel the furnaces. As shafts deepened to around 30 metres, miners encountered the water table and installed a steam engine to pump water, eventually reaching depths of roughly 150 metres. Kapunda’s ore averaged exceptionally high copper grades—over 20 percent—and the mine ranked fourth among South Australia’s historic copper producers, even as richer discoveries at Burra and later Moonta diminished its relative importance. By the early 1860s the richest underground ore was exhausted, prompting a transition to open-cut methods at workings such as the Charlotte Open Cut, part of a broader shift across the Kapunda site that also included the Sherwin and Main Open Cuts. This change allowed continued extraction of lower-grade but still valuable ore until the mine’s closure in 1878, when all equipment was sold and operations ceased.

Site: Iron Knob, Pastoral Unincorporated Area, South Australia
metadata: Iron Monarch Mine is the historic open-cut hematite operation that gave rise to the town of Iron Knob on South Australia’s Eyre Peninsula, long regarded as the birthplace of the Australian steel industry. Mining at Iron Monarch began in 1900, initially with ore hauled by bullock wagon to Port Augusta before the completion of the Broken Hill Tramway in 1901 enabled direct rail transport to Hummock Hill—later renamed Whyalla. By 1903, new wharves at Whyalla allowed ships to carry the exceptionally high-grade ore—typically 65 to 70 percent iron and once described in England as the richest known iron deposit in the world— to steelworks at Newcastle, and Port Kembla. Early extraction relied on hand loading and horse-drawn carts before the introduction of steam, diesel, and eventually electric shovels, with steam trains regularly hauling ore by 1918. Iron Monarch soon became the dominant producer within the Middleback Range, and by 1937 it accounted for most of the region’s output, then estimated at around two million tonnes per year. Over the twentieth century, about two hundred million tonnes of ore were mined from the Middleback Range’s major hematite bodies—particularly Iron Monarch and the nearby Iron Baron–Iron Prince deposits—which supplied the nation’s steel industry from 1915 to 1965. Quarrying at Iron Monarch ceased in 1998, triggering a sharp population decline in Iron Knob, which faced the prospect of becoming a ghost town after nearly a century of continuous mining.

Site: Mount Eba, Pastoral Unincorporated Area, South Australia
metadata: The Prominent Hill Mine is a major copper, silver, and gold operation located in north-west South Australia, approximately 130 kilometres south-east of Coober Pedy. It is an iron oxide copper gold style deposit situated within the Gawler Craton, hosted predominantly in a major fault zone where Hiltaba Suite granites overthrust a Mesoproterozoic volcano-sedimentary sequence. The mineralisation is dated at approximately one point five eight billion years old. Ore is extracted from the Malu and Ankata underground mining areas using open stoping methods, with the Malu mine extending beneath the base of the former open pit and the Ankata mine, opened in 2012, located about two kilometres west of the main operation. Processing is carried out using conventional crushing, grinding, and flotation, producing one of the highest-grade copper concentrates in the world. The mine was originally developed by Oxiana Limited, later operated by Oz Minerals, and is now owned by BHP. Production commenced in February 2009, and the operation was officially opened in May 2009 by South Australian Premier Mike Rann. Development required an investment of approximately one point one five billion Australian dollars and included construction of an open pit, processing plant, permanent workforce village, haulage road, power line, and bore field. At full production, the mine was projected to produce approximately 100,000 tonnes of copper and 115,000 ounces of gold in concentrate per year, with mine life expected to extend to 2030. The Malu open pit became the focus of controversy due to Aboriginal heritage concerns, particularly potential impacts on the sacred Magazine Hill site. These issues led to disputes with Traditional Owners and the Aboriginal Areas Protection Authority, alongside other business and safety challenges. Ultimately, the open pit closed in 2018 as mining operations transitioned entirely to underground extraction.

The Riley Creek Mine is a iron ore operation located approximately 18 kilometres west of Tullah in western Tasmania, near Sweeney Creek within the takayna or Tarkine region. The deposit consists mainly of hematite and maghemite developed within lateritic profiles formed by intense weathering. The project was approved in 2013 and developed by Venture Minerals as a small, short-life operation intended to exploit near-surface iron ore. Despite initial expectations, the mine produced only a single shipment of ore before operations were suspended and the site was placed on care and maintenance in 2021. This pause was attributed to volatile global shipping costs, high operating overheads, and the limited remaining mine life, which together undermined economic viability. In July 2024, Venture Minerals sold the Riley Creek Mine to Gold Valley Group for approximately 3 million Australian dollars.. The sale enabled Venture Minerals to redirect capital toward rare earth exploration projects, while transferring operational control and rehabilitation obligations to the new owner. The Riley Creek Mine has been controversial throughout its development due to its location within the takayna or Tarkine, an area widely regarded for its wilderness and potential World Heritage values. Environmental organisations, including the Bob Brown Foundation and Save the Tarkine, have opposed the project, arguing that mining is incompatible with long-term conservation and sustainable tourism. Concerns have focused on impacts to threatened species such as the Tasmanian devil and spotted quoll, resulting in legal challenges, protests, blockades, arrests, and the imposition of additional permit conditions. Critics have also characterised the project as a marginal, boom-and-bust operation, raising concerns that corporate failure could leave rehabilitation costs with taxpayers.

Site: Fosterville, City of Greater Bendigo, Victoria
metadata: The Central North Open Pit is part of the Fosterville Gold Mine. Fosterville Gold Mine is a major gold operation located east of Bendigo in Victoria and, as of 2025, is the state’s largest gold producer, noted for its high-grade ore and relatively low operating costs. Gold was first discovered in the area in 1894, with intermittent mining over more than a century, culminating in modern production beginning in 2005; by the end of 2024 the mine had produced about 4.4 million ounces of gold and is forecast to produce around 150,000 ounces in 2025. Proven and probable gold reserves are estimated at approximately 1.7 million ounces, underpinning the mine’s medium-term production outlook. Geologically, Fosterville is renowned for its exceptionally high-grade, structurally controlled gold mineralisation hosted within folded and faulted sedimentary rocks of the Bendigo Zone, with strong potential for further discoveries both underground and across the broader district. Ore is processed on site using conventional crushing and grinding followed by flotation, bacterial oxidation, and carbon-in-leach circuits to recover gold from sulphide mineralisation. The mine has passed through multiple owners, including Newmarket Gold, Kirkland Lake Gold, and since 2022 Agnico Eagle Mines Limited, reflecting its evolving geological understanding and economic significance. In 2024, Agnico Eagle signed a landmark agreement with the Dja Dja Wurrung People to formalise collaboration, employment, environmental stewardship, and financial contributions, setting an important precedent in Victoria. Despite its economic success, Fosterville remains politically and socially contentious, with community concerns over mining-induced seismic activity, environmental impacts on nearby forests and endangered species, risks to water quality and noise pollution, and stress on local residents. These issues have been examined through a formal Environmental Effects Statement inquiry, yet approvals for mine expansions such as tailings storage lifts have proceeded, intensifying debate between the company, government, and affected communities.
