metadata: The Leopold Lignite Mine (Tagebau Leopold) was a major open-cast brown coal mine in the Bitterfeld–Wolfen industrial region of Saxony-Anhalt, Germany, and forms part of the broader history of large-scale lignite exploitation that reshaped central Germany. Opened in 1908, the mine contributed to the rapid industrialization of the region by supplying fuel for power generation and briquette production. Like many lignite operations of its era, Leopold played a crucial economic role while profoundly transforming the landscape and local communities. Geologically, the mine exploited the Bitterfeld Seam, an Upper Oligocene coal layer approximately 24–28 million years old. This lignite seam typically measured 10–13 meters in thickness, locally reaching nearly 20 meters, and was interbedded with sands and clays deposited in swampy environments. The basin is also notable for the presence of Bitterfeld amber (succinite), fossilized tree resin found within the coal-bearing strata, giving the area additional paleontological significance. Early extraction relied on manual techniques, but by 1915 the introduction of steam shovels marked a decisive shift toward mechanized, high-volume mining. The mine ceased large-scale operations in 1934, having affected roughly 177 hectares and requiring the diversion of local watercourses such as the Strengbach. Its closure left a heavily degraded landscape, characteristic of central Germany’s lignite districts, where mining altered landforms, hydrology, and ecosystems. From the 1990s onward, the former Leopold pit became part of one of Europe’s most ambitious reclamation efforts. It was flooded and integrated into the Goitzsche Lake District, transforming former industrial scars into lakes used for recreation, wildlife habitats, and flood management. While often cited as a reclamation success, this transformation also highlights a broader controversy: the long-term environmental costs of lignite mining, including greenhouse gas emissions, displacement of communities, and irreversible landscape change, which reclamation can mitigate but never fully undo.

metadata: The Garzweiler open-cast mine is one of Europe’s largest lignite (brown coal) surface mines, located in North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany, and operated by RWE Power. Active since the mid-twentieth century, it currently covers about 48 square kilometres and forms part of the long-exploited Rhenish lignite mining district. Garzweiler supplies fuel to major nearby power plants, notably Neurath and Niederaußem, making it a cornerstone of regional electricity generation but also a major source of carbon dioxide emissions. Geologically, the mine exploits thick Tertiary lignite seams of the Lower Rhine Basin, formed from peat accumulation in extensive swamp environments during the Miocene. These coal layers are interbedded with sands and clays and lie close to the surface, enabling large-scale open-pit extraction using bucket-wheel excavators. Annual production has typically ranged between 20 and 25 million tonnes of lignite, transported by conveyor belts and rail directly to power stations. Proven reserves exceed one billion tonnes, although extraction volumes are now being reduced in line with Germany’s coal exit policy. Garzweiler is internationally controversial due to its social, environmental, and climate impacts. Since the 1960s, more than 30,000 people have been resettled, with entire villages demolished to make way for the expanding pit. Infrastructure has also been heavily affected, including the permanent removal and rerouting of major motorways. The mine has become a focal point for climate activism, with mass protests in 2015 and 2023 highlighting its role as one of Europe’s largest single sources of carbon dioxide emissions. Although Germany has committed to ending lignite mining at Garzweiler by 2030, court rulings and energy-security measures have allowed continued expansion in the short term, most notably the destruction of the village of Lützerath.

metadata: The Baboto South Open Pit is a satellite gold mine within the world-class Loulo–Gounkoto Mining Complex in western Mali, located in the Kéniéba Cercle of the Kayes Region near the border with Senegal. It lies within the historic Bambouk gold province, an area mined since antiquity during the Ghana and Mali Empires, and today forms part of one of Africa’s most productive industrial gold districts. Geologically, Baboto is situated in the West Mali Gold Belt, a 180-kilometre-long mineralized corridor controlled by the Senegal–Mali shear zone, a major crustal-scale structure of the West African Craton. Gold mineralization occurs in steeply west-dipping hydrothermal structures within the Kenieba–Kedougou Inlier. The Baboto pit primarily exploits oxide and transitional ores, which are softer and easier to process than the refractory ores mined underground at the Yalea and Gara deposits. Recent drilling has intersected higher-grade mineralization at depth, indicating continued exploration potential beyond current open-pit limits. Operationally, Baboto is managed as part of the Loulo–Gounkoto complex by Barrick Gold Corporation (eighty percent ownership) in partnership with the State of Mali (twenty percent). Reopened in early 2024 and mined by a Malian contractor, the pit supplies ore to centralized processing facilities. Today, Baboto supports large-scale industrial gold production while existing alongside widespread artisanal mining, which remains extremely dangerous—as tragically illustrated in February 2025, when forty-three people, mostly women, were killed in the collapse of an artisanal gold mine near Kéniéba. While the Loulo–Gounkoto complex contributes over one billion United States dollars annually to Mali’s economy and promotes renewable energy initiatives such as large solar power installations, it also highlights persistent challenges. These include the stark contrast between regulated industrial mining and informal artisanal practices, ongoing environmental pressures, and the profound social risks faced by local communities whose livelihoods remain closely tied to gold extraction.

Site: Loulo Mining area, Kéniéba Cercle, Kayes Region
Loulo Mining area, Kéniéba Cercle, Kayes Region
metadata: The Gounkoto mine is a major gold mining operation forming the core of the Loulo–Gounkoto gold complex, one of Africa’s most significant gold-producing centers, located in western Mali along the Falémé River on the border with Senegal. Together with the adjacent Loulo mine, it is owned by Barrick Gold Corporation (eighty percent) and the State of Mali (twenty percent), and includes both large open-pit and underground operations. Since entering production in 2011, Gounkoto has helped make the Loulo–Gounkoto complex a world-class producer, contributing more than seven hundred thousand ounces of gold in 2024 and over one billion United States dollars to Mali’s economy in 2023. Mining is currently planned to continue through at least 2037, following phased open-pit depletion and underground expansion. Geologically, the Gounkoto mine lies within the Kédougou–Kéniéba Inlier, a Birimian-age gold province of the West African Craton renowned for structurally controlled mineralization. Gold is concentrated along major shear zones and associated hydrothermal alteration systems. At the broader complex, mineralization at Yalea occurs in brecciated quartzites along the Yalea Shear, while the Gara deposit is hosted in strongly tourmaline-altered greywacke. Combined proven and probable reserves at Loulo–Gounkoto are estimated at approximately fifty-seven million tonnes grading about four grams per tonne gold, containing roughly seven million ounces. Beyond geology and production, the Gounkoto mine illustrates the political, social, and environmental challenges surrounding large-scale gold mining in Mali. In early 2025 the mine became the focus of a major dispute when the Malian government seized gold, blocked exports, and temporarily took control of operations amid disagreements over taxation and the revised mining code. Although a settlement later in 2025 allowed a gradual restart, the episode highlighted ongoing tensions between national resource sovereignty, foreign mining investment, and the economic dependence of the region on industrial gold production.

Koashva Mt, Khibiny Massif, Murmansk Oblast
metadata: The Koashva Open Pit is a vast open-cast mine in northern Russia, near the town of Kirovsk on the Kola Peninsula, and it plays a crucial role in global fertilizer production. By extracting phosphate-rich rock that is essential for making agricultural fertilizers, the mine supports food production far beyond the Arctic region where it is located. Operated by Apatit Joint Stock Company, part of the PhosAgro group, Koashva is one of the most important mining sites in the region and a cornerstone of Russia’s fertilizer industry.phosphate raw material for fertilizer production, supplying the basis for phosphorus-based agricultural fertilizers. Geologically, Koashva lies within the Khibiny Massif, a roughly 370-million-year-old domed alkaline intrusion approximately 40 kilometers in diameter. Mineralization occurs mainly within apatite–nepheline syenites associated with an arc-shaped ijolite–urtite body up to 11 kilometers long. The ores typically contain 45–50 percent apatite and 35–40 percent nepheline, with aegirine, titanite, titanomagnetite, and numerous rare silicates as accessories. Ultra-agpaitic pegmatites make Koashva a type locality for dozens of minerals and one of the most mineralogically diverse sites on Earth. Ore bodies dip steeply and extend from more than 1,000 meters above sea level to several hundred meters below. Beyond its geological significance, Koashva exemplifies the environmental and engineering risks of deep open-pit mining in Arctic conditions. Complex hydrogeology leads to severe seasonal water inflows, bench deformation, and extensive dewatering that disrupts regional groundwater systems. Increasing pit depth has altered stress regimes in the rock mass, contributing to heightened seismicity across the central Kola Peninsula. Mine-closure planning is therefore a major unresolved issue, with long-term concerns over slope stability, aquifer protection, and landscape rehabilitation in a sensitive subarctic environment.

The Kirovogorsk Open Pit is a large iron ore mine located just west of the town of Olenegorsk on Russia’s Kola Peninsula, and it has been the economic foundation of the town since the late 1940s. Mining began in 1949 alongside the construction of Olenegorsk as a planned company town serving nearby strip mines and processing plants. Today, the mine remains a key supplier of iron ore concentrate to the Russian steel industry, particularly to Severstal’s Cherepovets steelworks, and continues to shape the region’s industrial identity. Geologically, the Kirovogorsk deposit belongs to an ancient banded iron formation formed more than two billion years ago. The ore consists mainly of magnetite and hematite hosted in hard quartzite and gneiss. While the site is primarily industrial rather than collectible in character, a modest range of associated minerals has been identified, reflecting the complex Precambrian geology of the Kola Peninsula. Beyond mining, Olenegorsk occupies a strategically sensitive landscape. The town lies along the Saint Petersburg–Murmansk railway and near major military installations, including the Olenya air base and the Olenegorsk early-warning radar station. Intensive mining across the region has also created long-term environmental challenges. Large-scale dewatering of open pits has altered groundwater flow, lowered water tables, degraded water quality, and affected soils and vegetation. These impacts are shared with other mining operations across Murmansk Oblast and remain a central issue in planning mine closures and future development in the Arctic environment.

The Nyurbinskaya mine is one of the largest and most productive diamond mines in Russia and globally, located in the Sakha Republic (Yakutia) within the Nakyn Kimberlite Field. Discovered relatively recently in 1996, it represents a “new generation” of large diamond deposits compared with the historic Mirny and Udachnaya mines. Operated by ALROSA through its Nyurba Mining and Processing Division, the mine has estimated reserves of about 67.8 million carats and an annual production capacity of nearly 7 million carats. Together with nearby deposits, it contributes roughly 15–20 percent of ALROSA’s total diamond output. Geologically, the Nyurbinskaya pipe is distinctive among Yakutian kimberlites. It is deeply buried beneath Triassic and Jurassic sedimentary rocks, meaning it lacked obvious surface indicators and was discovered using advanced geophysical exploration methods. The pipe has a rounded, ellipsoidal shape that narrows significantly with depth and splits into two separate ore bodies below about 300 meters, divided by a wedge of barren rock. The kimberlite is considered high grade, with a high diamond concentration per tonne, and contains well-preserved mantle xenoliths that are of considerable scientific interest. The broader Nakyn hub also includes the Botuobinskaya and Maiskaya pipes, as well as diamond-bearing alluvial placer deposits derived from ancient erosion of the kimberlites. Beyond geology and production, the mine operates in a sensitive environmental and social context. It lies in a remote region traditionally used by the Sakha people, and concerns have periodically been raised about impacts on water systems linked to the Vilyuy River basin. The operation relies on fly-in, fly-out shift work due to its isolation, while remaining a major regional employer and taxpayer. Balancing industrial output, environmental protection, and Indigenous interests remains an ongoing challenge at Nyurbinskaya.

metadata: The Loolekop pipe at the center of the Phalaborwa Igneous Complex is globally unique as the only large carbonatite-hosted copper mine in the world. Located in Limpopo Province, South Africa, the deposit is the economic and geological heart of a vast ancient volcanic system that also yields phosphate, iron, vermiculite, and numerous strategic by-products. Palabora has been central to South Africa’s copper and fertilizer industries for decades, while the nearby town of Phalaborwa was effectively created to service the mine. Geologically, Loolekop represents the deeply eroded throat of a volcano formed approximately 2.06 billion years ago. In cross-section, the complex resembles a layered cylinder: an enormous outer zone of pyroxenite containing significant phosphate; a thinner inner ring of foskorite, rich in magnetite and apatite; and a small but exceptionally valuable carbonatite core that hosts most of the copper mineralization. Although the foskorite and carbonatite together make up only a few percent of the total complex, they contain a disproportionate share of its economic value, making Palabora one of the most intensively studied alkaline igneous systems on Earth. Mining history at Loolekop long predates industrial extraction. Indigenous Iron Age communities exploited naturally exposed magnetite on the hill, establishing furnaces and producing tools and weapons centuries before European contact. Oral histories of the baPhalaborwa people describe migration from present-day Zimbabwe and conflict with earlier inhabitants, followed by settlement near Loole Kop because of its rich iron resources—reflected in the name Phalaborwa, often translated as “better than the south.” In the modern era, large-scale mining introduced new forms of conflict, including land dispossession, labor migration, and environmental pressure. Today, debates continue around water use, pollution, and cultural heritage, especially given the mine’s proximity to Kruger National Park, highlighting enduring tensions between industrial wealth, indigenous histories, and environmental stewardship.

Site: Joe Morolong Local Municipality, John Taolo Gaetsewe District Municipality, Northern Cape
metadata: The Adams Pit is an integral component of the Mamatwan Mine, one of the largest open-pit manganese operations in the world, located in the Kalahari Manganese Field of South Africa’s Northern Cape. Originally developed as an extraction pit, Adams Pit has transitioned into a specialized facility focused on waste storage, water management, and long-term rehabilitation, reflecting the mine’s shift toward closure planning and environmental control. Although the broader Mamatwan operation has been active since 1963, Adams Pit is no longer used for ore extraction. It now functions primarily as an in-pit tailings storage facility, receiving manganese slurry generated during ore processing. Historically, it also served as a disposal site for sinter de-dust, low-grade ore, and general industrial waste such as rubble and obsolete conveyor components. In addition, the pit acts as a reservoir within a closed-loop system that manages excess water pumped from active pits and processing areas. Since 2024, reclamation efforts have begun, with stored materials being reprocessed or stabilized as part of long-term rehabilitation plans. Geologically, Adams Pit lies within the Hotazel Formation, dated to approximately 2.2–2.4 billion years, and hosts so-called Mamatwan-type ore, a carbonate-rich manganese lutite. The ore is medium to low grade, typically containing 30–38 percent manganese, and is dominated by hausmannite and braunite, with associated carbonates such as ankerite, calcite, and manganocalcite, as well as secondary oxides like todorokite and manganite. Operated by Hotazel Manganese Mines, a consortium including South32, Anglo American, and Broad-Based Black Economic Empowerment partners, the pit remains controversial. Environmental groups continue to scrutinize the long-term groundwater risks of slurry disposal in the sensitive Kalahari aquifers. At the same time, rising tensions around illegal sugilite mining highlight broader conflicts between industrial operators and local communities, who often feel excluded from the economic benefits of valuable by-product minerals.

metadata: The Lavender Pit is a former large open-pit copper mine at Bisbee, Arizona, adjacent to the historic Copper Queen Mine, and represents a turning point in twentieth-century copper mining. Opened in 1950 by the Phelps Dodge Corporation, it was designed to make Bisbee’s previously unprofitable low-grade copper ores economically viable through large-scale open-pit methods. The pit was named after Harrison M. Lavender, a senior executive of Phelps Dodge who championed this approach. Mining continued until 1974, yielding roughly 600,000 tons of copper, with gold and silver as by-products, from ore averaging about 0.7 percent copper. Geologically, the Lavender Pit exposes a classic cross-section of the Warren Mining District. Mineralization consists mainly of low-grade disseminated chalcocite, with localized copper and zinc sulfides, hosted in a brecciated intrusive porphyry plug adjacent to altered Paleozoic limestones along the Dividend Fault. The competent limestone and porphyry host rocks allowed the pit to be excavated with unusually steep walls. The open pit covers about 300 acres and reaches a depth of roughly 900 feet (274 metres), making it one of the most visually striking mining features in the American Southwest. The vividly colored pit walls display iron oxides, jarosite, and copper carbonates such as malachite and azurite. Beyond copper, the Lavender Pit is famous for Bisbee Blue turquoise, an exceptionally hard and richly colored gemstone recovered as a by-product. Today, the pit’s legacy is complex. Its expansion led to the demolition of much of the Lowell neighborhood, creating a lasting social scar, and decades of mining caused acid mine drainage that polluted local waterways. Since the early twenty-first century, extensive remediation has reduced environmental impacts, and the Lavender Pit has become a dramatic landmark central to Bisbee’s post-mining identity as a heritage and tourism destination.

Site: Banner Mining District, Gila County, Arizona
metadata: The Christmas Mine is a historic copper mining complex in the Banner Mining District of Gila County, Arizona, located about 13 km north of Winkelman and 35 km south of Globe. Copper was first discovered in the area in 1880, but sustained mining began in 1905 after legal disputes over land within the San Carlos Apache Reservation were resolved by a federal boundary change in 1902. The mine takes its name from Christmas Eve, when prospector George B. Chittenden re-staked the claims. Between 1905 and 1943, the mine produced approximately 25,000 tonnes of copper, valued at over 10 million United States dollars, with minor silver and gold by-products. Mining continued intermittently until final closure in 1982, after a transition from underground to open-pit methods. Geologically, the Christmas Mine is a classic contact metasomatic (skarn) deposit. Copper mineralization occurs mainly in gently dipping Paleozoic limestones (Devonian to Permian in age) that were intruded by a small quartz diorite stock. The interaction between the intrusive body and favorable limestone beds produced garnet-rich skarns hosting copper sulfides. Chalcopyrite is the dominant ore mineral, accompanied by bornite, chalcocite, and oxidized copper minerals, with gangue minerals including garnet, quartz, magnetite, and unreplaced limestone. Most production came from tabular replacement bodies in the Naco Limestone, with smaller deposits in the Escabrosa and Martin limestones. Beyond geology, the Christmas Mine strongly shaped local social history. A company town briefly flourished, reaching about 1,000 residents in the early 1930s, notable for its orderly character and famous Christmas-themed postmark. The mine is also scientifically important as the type locality for rare minerals such as apachite, junitoite, and ruizite. Today, the site is inactive, owned by Freeport-McMoRan, and closed to the public, standing as a layered record of mining, community formation, and mineralogical significance in central Arizona.

Kramer Borate deposit, Boron, Kern County, California
metadata: The Rio Tinto Boron Mine, located near the town of Boron in California’s Mojave Desert, is the largest open-pit mine in the state and the largest borax mine in the world, supplying roughly 30–40 percent of global borate demand. Discovered in 1913 during water drilling, the deposit was developed by the Pacific Coast Borax Company, later U.S. Borax, and has been operated by Rio Tinto since its acquisition. Mining began underground in 1927 and transitioned fully to open-pit methods in 1957. Today, the mine produces about one million tonnes of refined borates annually, with reserves sufficient to support production into the early 2040s. Geologically, the deposit formed about 16 million years ago in a non-marine structural basin occupied by a permanent shallow lake fed by sodium- and boron-rich thermal springs. The primary mineral, borax, precipitated directly from lake waters. Borate minerals, including borax, kernite, and ulexite, occur within the Middle Miocene Kramer beds, where a borate-rich core facies is completely enveloped by ulexite-bearing shales. The deposit is structurally controlled, bounded in part by the Western Borax Fault. Beyond its geology and scale, the Boron Mine has had significant environmental and labor impacts. The operation occupies a vast industrial landscape of roughly 54 square kilometers, including the open pit, tailings piles, settling ponds, and processing facilities, permanently transforming a fragile desert ecosystem. The high waste-to-ore ratio has produced enormous tailings terraces, while continuous dewatering and processing place long-term stress on scarce groundwater resources. Although Rio Tinto has introduced water recycling systems and converted heavy machinery to renewable diesel, critics note that the mine remains highly energy- and water-intensive, with cumulative impacts that are difficult to fully mitigate in an arid environment. Labor relations at Boron have also been a major point of controversy. In 2010, Rio Tinto locked out more than 560 union workers for several months after they rejected contract terms that would have reduced pensions, weakened seniority protections, and expanded outsourcing.

metadata: The Maybell Pit is a former uranium mining and milling site in Moffat County, northwestern Colorado, located at an elevation of approximately 1,920 meters above sea level. The small town of Maybell lies about 8 kilometers to the southwest. The site forms part of the historic Maybell Mining District, which was developed rapidly during the Cold War uranium boom of the 1950s. Uranium mining in the district began in 1955, following discoveries made the previous year. A uranium mill was established in 1955 by Trace Elements Corporation and taken over in 1957 by Union Carbide Corporation. Between 1957 and 1964, the mill processed approximately 2.36 million tonnes of ore, producing large volumes of radioactive tailings. These tailings consisted mainly of sandy material left after uranium extraction. Milling operations ceased in 1964, and partial stabilization efforts began in the early 1970s. Geologically, uranium mineralization occurs in lenticular, sandstone-hosted ore bodies roughly 300 meters long and 9 meters thick, within sandstones of the Browns Park Formation. The region contains widespread natural uranium mineralization, and groundwater commonly shows elevated concentrations of uranium, arsenic, cadmium, lead, selenium, and molybdenum. Groundwater occurs at depths ranging from about 11 to 91 meters understood to be unconfined and of generally low yield. Comprehensive remediation began in 1995 under the U.S. Department of Energy. All contaminated materials—including mill tailings, demolished structures, and impacted soils—were consolidated into an engineered on-site disposal cell, completed in 1998. A second area, the Maybell West Site, had been used between 1975 and 1982 for heap-leach uranium extraction; reclamation there was completed in 2005, stabilizing roughly 1.8 million tonnes of tailings.

metadata: The Adi Rassi deposit, 30 kilometres south of Asmara, occurs in the southern section of the Adi Rassi–Kodatu mineralised trend within the Asmara District. It contains an estimated resource of 16 million tonnes at 0.5 percent copper and 0.3 grams per tonne gold. Copper, gold and silver mineralisation is associated with foliation planes, breccia zones and quartz veins developed along a major north–south shear zone traceable for more than 3 kilometres. Mining at Adi Rassi began in the 1930s, when Italian operators excavated a series of 10 to 20 metre deep open pits along the shear zone, mainly targeting oxide copper in the South Hill zone. The deposit was again exploited briefly in the 1980s, but operations were halted by military conflict during Eritrea’s struggle for independence from Ethiopia. Following renewed border hostilities between 1998 and 2000, modern exploration of the Asmara District resumed, focusing on volcanic-hosted massive sulphide copper, zinc, silver and gold systems and confirming Adi Rassi as a significant shear-hosted copper–gold target adjacent to the established volcanic-hosted massive sulphide deposits. These campaigns have progressively refined resources, geology and structural controls on mineralisation.

The Debarwa deposit lies about 30 km south-southwest of central Asmara, the capital of Eritrea. It is hosted by an overturned, moderately to steeply dipping sequence of variably to intensely altered tholeiitic basaltic and rhyolitic volcanic rocks, with minor chemical sedimentary units. The exposed rusty, iron-rich, weathered caps at Debarwa attracted some of the earliest drilling in the district, both before and after Eritrean independence in 1993. The deposit was mined briefly in the 1980s, but operations ceased when military conflict intensified during Eritrea’s struggle for independence from Ethiopia. After renewed border hostilities between 1998 and 2000 subsided, systematic exploration of the Asmara district resumed. Debarwa now forms part of the Asmara Project, an advanced copper-zinc-gold-silver development that includes four main deposits—Debarwa, Emba Derho, Adi Nefas and Gupo—and two pipeline deposits, Adi Rassi and Kodadu. Most operating and exploration companies involved are Western, particularly from Canada, Australia and the UK, and their senior technical expertise largely reflects these origins. Eritreans are employed mainly in supplies, accounting, clerical roles, machinery operation and technical support. However, the bulk of the manual workforce is drawn from National Service conscripts, who have been reported to work under highly exploitative conditions, with inadequate food and poor living quarters.

Emba Derho is a large volcanogenic massive sulphide deposit hosting gold, silver, copper and zinc, located west-north-west of Asmara. It belongs to the Asmara Project, which comprises four advanced deposits—Debarwa, Emba Derho, Adi Nefas and Gupo—and two pipeline deposits, Adi Rassi and Kodadu. Mineralisation at Emba Derho extends over about 1,250 m of strike and 850 m in width, while Adi Nefas occurs as an elongated north-northeast-trending, steeply east-dipping massive sulphide layer within a bimodal sequence of metavolcanic and metasedimentary rocks. The project is planned in three phases. Phase I began with open-pit mining at Debarwa and shipment of direct shipping ore through the port of Massawa. Phase II adds a gold heap-leach operation on near-surface ore and mining of supergene copper from Debarwa and Emba Derho, processed at a central 2 million tonnes per year flotation plant at Emba Derho, with copper concentrate plus gold and silver by-products exported via Massawa. Phase III will treat primary copper and zinc ores from Emba Derho and nearby sites, producing copper and zinc concentrates. Power is generated onsite from diesel and medium-fuel-oil generators, water is largely recycled, yet local communities question the benefits and landscape and agricultural impacts.

The Gupo mine lies within the Asmara District of Eritrea, where volcanic-hosted massive sulphide deposits of copper, zinc, silver and gold occur close to the capital, Asmara. Gupo itself is predominantly a gold deposit hosted in highly deformed, strongly foliated mafic flows and mafic tuffs, intercalated and crosscut by thin quartz-phyric felsic flows and subordinate dykes. The highest-grade gold mineralisation occurs in crystalline, coarse-grained pyrite within quartz veins, while lower-grade gold is associated with medium- to coarse-grained euhedral to sub-euhedral pyrite in sericite-altered selvages. Gupo forms part of the Asmara Project, held by the Asmara Mining Share Company, a 60:40 joint venture between the Chinese company SRBM and state-owned ENAMCO. The project comprises four deposits—Emba Derho, Adi Nefas, Gupo and Debarwa. In the first phase, high-grade copper ore will be mined by open pit at Debarwa, containerised, and transported 120 km to the port of Massawa for shipment to a Chinese smelter. At full production, the project is expected to operate for 17 years and annually yield about 29,000 t copper, 83,000 t zinc, 42,000 oz gold and 1 million oz silver in its first eight years. Concerns have been voiced about the benefit of these operations to the local inhabitants and the impact on the surrounding landscape and agriculture.

The Hambok mine, a major silver- and zinc-bearing site, is one of several operations developed along Eritrea’s volcanic massive sulfide belt, where mineral extraction has a long history. After a suspension of all mineral exploration was lifted in early 2005, the Government revised its mining framework, increasing its maximum equity stake in projects to 30% from 2006 onward. Eritrea’s mining code, modelled on that of Australia’s Northern Territory, stipulates royalties of 5% on precious metals and 3% on base metals, alongside a 35% corporate tax rate. Within this framework, Sanu Resources emerged as the leading exploration landholder, with licences covering more than 2,600 km², focused largely on Hambok, its most recent volcanic massive sulfide discovery in the polymetallic belt. Drilling—7,382 m across 43 diamond drill holes—confirmed extensive massive sulfide mineralisation over more than 1,050 m of strike length and 300 m downdip, remaining open in all directions and indicating significant copper-zinc potential. Since 2012, the Hambok copper-zinc deposit has been part of the Bisha Mining Company, 60% owned by Nevsun Resources and 40% by the Eritrean National Mining Company.

The Harena Mine is an open copper and zinc operation located close to the better-known Bisha site in Eritrea’s Gash-Barka region, north of the provincial capital Barentu. Developed as a satellite deposit to the northwest of the Bisha main zone, Harena is estimated, as of 2023, to contain proven and probable reserves of about 3.09 million tonnes of zinc at an average grade of 3.75% and 800,000 tonnes of copper at 0.97%. The operation is run by Zijin-Bisha Mining, an Eritrean company established to develop and operate the Bisha district mines, and jointly owned by China’s Zijin Mining Group, with a 55% stake, and the state-owned Eritrean National Mining Corporation, with 45%. Although the region is highly mineralised, it is also the agricultural heartland of Eritrea and was once heavily forested before widespread deforestation transformed the highland plateau. Politically, the country has been ruled by a single party, the People’s Front for Democracy and Justice, the successor to the Eritrean People’s Liberation Front, since independence in 1991, and continues to operate under a transitional government elected in 1993, with national elections originally scheduled for 2001 repeatedly postponed. This political context shapes governance of mining and land use practices.

The south-western part of Eritrea is one of the most prospective gold-bearing regions in the country. The Augaro mine developed on a series of quartz veins and stringers, some showing sulphide mineralisation, formed in a low-pressure zone related to a regional shear system. The wider area hosts hundreds of small historic mines and operations that were active during the Italian occupation, which ended in 1941 with the East African campaign of World War II. Augaro was reportedly the most productive gold mine in Eritrea, and during Italian colonisation mercury amalgamation was widely used, with tailings dumped without proper waste management. Today, gold mining is increasingly informal and largely outside federal control; some projects have been overtaken by illegal exploration, extraction and smuggling, often in collusion with state and non-state actors. These activities generally ignore sustainable mining practices and worker health. A major environmental and health concern is mercury release from artisanal and small-scale mining, including in-situ evaporation from tailings near farming areas in Eritrea’s hot climate. Land degradation from unrehabilitated pits is substantial, and because small-scale mining is largely informal and miners’ awareness is limited, land reclamation remains extremely rare.

Site: Balangero, Metropolitan City of Turin, Piedmont
metadata: The Balangero asbestos mine, also known as the San Vittore or Poggio San Vittore mine, was a vast open-pit chrysotile asbestos operation located on the municipal territories of Balangero and Corio, about 30 kilometres north of Turin in northern Italy. Active from 1918 until its closure in 1990, it was the largest asbestos mine in Europe and at times one of the three largest worldwide. The mine dominated the local economy for decades, producing asbestos fibres, powders, and fibre cement, but left behind a severely contaminated landscape of roughly 400 hectares. Following Italy’s national asbestos ban in 1992, the site was permanently closed, and mineral collecting is now strictly forbidden. Geologically, the mine exploited a serpentinite body of about 7 square kilometres that forms a satellite of the Lanzo ultramafic massif. This body consists mainly of antigorite serpentinite with relics of original mantle peridotite and is tectonically associated with the Sesia–Lanzo Zone. Chrysotile asbestos occurs within these serpentinised ultramafic rocks. The Balangero serpentinite is also the type locality for the rare mineral balangeroite alongside more than 100 other mineral species identified during mining. The mine is infamous for its environmental and health legacy. Decades of poorly controlled extraction released asbestos fibres into air, soil, and water, leading to elevated rates of mesothelioma among workers and nearby residents. After closure, unstable tailings dumps posed a major landslide and contamination risk. Beginning in the 1990s, Italian authorities launched a large-scale remediation program, including slope stabilisation, water filtration, vegetation restoration, and continuous air monitoring. While reclamation remains ongoing, Balangero is now regarded as a rare example of how one of Europe’s most toxic industrial sites might be progressively transformed into a safer, semi-natural landscape. Despite these efforts, however, persistent asbestos dust remains a danger and hazardous to visitors today.

Site: Traversella, Metropolitan City of Turin, Piedmont
metadata: The Traversella Mine, located in the Val Chiusella area of Piedmont about 50 kilometres north of Turin, Italy, is a historic iron mining site exploited from the late Middle Ages until its final closure in 1971. Together with the nearby Brosso mine, Traversella was one of the most important iron producers in the region and a major driver of local economic development. Mining activity intensified during the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries with rising industrial demand, experienced periodic closures due to foreign competition, and briefly revived under autarkic policies in the 1930s. After decades of declining profitability, the mine was permanently shut down, leaving behind extensive underground workings and surface infrastructure that are now preserved as industrial heritage within a regional ecomuseum. Geologically, Traversella represents a classic contact-metasomatic (skarn) deposit formed at the interface between the Traversella pluton and metamorphic host rocks of the Sesia–Lanzo Zone, including gneisses, micaschists, and marbles. The pluton ranges in composition from quartz diorite to monzodiorite and acted both as a heat source and as the origin of metasomatizing fluids. Iron mineralisation occurred mainly as magnetite within skarn bodies developed in the contact aureole, with later exploitation of pyrite and chalcopyrite for sulfuric acid production. Traversella is globally significant as the type locality for ferro-ferri-hornblende, an amphibole mineral first described in the early twentieth century. Beyond its scientific importance, the mine was associated with serious risks and social tensions. Centuries of unplanned tunnel excavation led to frequent collapses and loss of life. Economic dependence on mining made the community vulnerable to market downturns, while environmental legacies from waste and abandoned workings remain topics of debate. A storied pathway, the "path of the souls", originating from ancient oral traditions of the people of Valchiusella, refers to the mysterious engravings on the rock that meet along a path that leads from Traversella to Piani di Cappia. A legend tells of a story of how souls of the dead passed through this passage way and for this reason, the shepherds could not place the fences around their flocks, and so that the animals wouldn’t be frightened at the sight of the souls passing by. Another legend tells of the elderly, who wanted to end their lives, would let themselves fall off the steep path.

The Kreas Mine is a historic gold mining site located near Alagna Valsesia, at the head of the valley within the Monte Rosa massif in the Italian Alps. It forms part of the broader Pestarena–Val Quarazza–Val Quarazzola gold district, one of the most important historic gold-producing areas in Italy. Mining activity began in earnest in the late sixteenth century under local families and later passed to noble and state control, including the House of Savoy. In the eighteenth century, purpose-built service buildings were constructed to support operations, some of which still survive. Gold mining declined by the twentieth century, and although a brief revival was attempted in the 1950s, it ended in bankruptcy. The site remained active longer as a feldspar mine supplying the glass and ceramics industries. Today, Kreas is being redeveloped as a mining park within the Sesia Val Grande Geopark. Geologically, the Kreas Mine is hosted within high-grade metamorphic rocks of the Upper Penninic domain of the Monte Rosa Unit, part of the axial Alpine chain. Mineralisation occurs in a system of auriferous quartz–sulfide veins, with a dominant west-northwest to east-southeast orientation. Gold is primarily associated with pyrite, accompanied by quartz, galena, sphalerite, and stibnite. The Kreas central vein represents the main ore body, while nearby workings such as the San Maurizio mine exploit smaller subsidiary veins. Later exploitation targeted feldspar-rich lenses within the metamorphic host rocks, which proved more economically viable than gold. The mine’s history is marked by controversy and risk. Unplanned tunnelling led to structural instability and collapses, some of which persist today. Mid-twentieth-century feldspar mining destroyed significant early modern gold workings, prompting criticism from historians and archaeologists. Social tensions also arose from outside labour entering the traditionally Walser community. Today, a tourism development and heritage project seeks to promote the region’s history with a larger audience.

Site: San Lorenzo, Valdieri, Cuneo Province, Piedmont
metadata: The Carbocalcio Quarry, also known historically as the Marmorea or San Lorenzo Quarry, is a large limestone quarry located on the southern slope of Monte La Piastra in the Gesso Valley near Valdieri, Piedmont, Italy. Opened in 1722 as the Real Cava Marmorea, it was originally owned by the House of Savoy and supplied prestigious ornamental stone known as Bardiglio di Valdieri for royal and civic architecture. During the twentieth century, declining demand for decorative marble led to a shift in production. Today, the quarry is operated by Carbocalcio Cuneese S.p.A. and focuses on the extraction of limestone for the manufacture of ground calcium carbonate powders used in paper, plastics, paints, and other industrial applications, making it an important local economic asset. Geologically, the quarry exploits recrystallised carbonate rocks of the Dauphinois sedimentary succession. These include the Lower Cretaceous Lausa Limestone, the Aptian–Cenomanian Marne Nere unit, and the Upper Cretaceous Puriac Limestone. Recrystallisation mainly affects the Lausa Limestone, which occurs as very pure white marble with subordinate grey banded varieties composed of calcite mosaics. Locally, silicate-bearing levels occur, containing phyllosilicates, potassium feldspar, albite, quartz, and accessory minerals such as pyrite. Dolostone bodies crosscut by quartz-, calcite-, and ankerite-filled veins record brittle deformation during ductile flow of surrounding limestones. Recent studies suggest these “Valdieri marbles” formed through localized hydrothermal recrystallisation during Oligocene tectonism rather than regional metamorphism. The quarry’s continued operation has generated debate. Its location near the Maritime Alps Natural Park raises concerns about landscape impact, slope stability, groundwater flow, and proximity to significant archaeological sites, including Bronze Age necropolises.

The Furtei Mine, also known as Monte Santu Miali, is a closed gold mine near the village of Furtei in southern Sardinia, Italy. It was historically significant as Italy’s first and only modern gold mine and represented a late-20th-century attempt to revive Sardinia’s long tradition of metal mining. Operated by Sardinia Gold Mining, a joint venture involving Australian investors and the regional government, the mine was active mainly between 1997 and 2003. During this period, approximately 4.2 tonnes of gold were produced, largely from oxidized ores processed by cyanide heap leaching. Mining ceased abruptly when oxide reserves were exhausted and deeper sulfide ores proved technically and economically challenging, leaving the site abandoned and transferring remediation responsibility to public authorities. Geologically, Furtei is a high-sulfidation epithermal gold deposit related to Oligo–Miocene volcanic activity. Mineralization is structurally controlled and concentrated along tectonic zones affected by intense hydrothermal alteration. The ore assemblage is dominated by iron–copper–arsenic–antimony sulfides and sulfosalts, including enargite, luzonite, pyrite, tetrahedrite, and tennantite, with gold occurring both as native metal and in minerals such as calaverite. An oxidized cap rich in iron oxy-hydroxides and jarosite overlies deeper sulfide zones and was the main target of early mining. After closure, serious environmental and safety issues emerged. Cyanide-bearing tailings, acid mine drainage, and arsenic-rich wastes have contaminated soils and waters, reflecting broader patterns of mining-related arsenic dispersion in Sardinia. Acidic drainage leaches toxic metals into nearby catchments, posing risks to ecosystems and agriculture on the Campidano plain. Furtei has since become a prominent case study of the enduring environmental legacy of aggressive modern gold mining in Europe.

The Su Pedrosu Quarry, also known as the Monte Pedrosu Quarry, is an active industrial extraction site located in the municipality of Bessude in the Logudoro region of northern Sardinia, Italy. It is a key supplier of construction aggregates for the province of Sassari and the wider northern Sardinian market. Operated by Conglomerati Bituminosi S.r.l., the quarry provides high-durability materials used in road construction, concrete production, and hydraulic infrastructure, benefiting from its proximity to the SS 131 Carlo Felice highway, which allows efficient regional distribution. Geologically, the quarry exploits volcanic rocks belonging to the Logudoro Volcanic Field, formed during intense Miocene to Pliocene volcanic activity. The extracted material is primarily andesite, more precisely basaltic andesite, valued for its mechanical strength and resistance to abrasion. These volcanic rocks are processed into standardized construction aggregates that comply with European Union regulatory requirements and carry European Conformity certification. The stone is particularly suitable for bituminous conglomerates used in asphalt, where durability and long-term performance are critical. The volcanic origin of the rock reflects Sardinia’s complex tectono-magmatic history, which has produced extensive volcanic plateaus across the Logudoro area. Beyond extraction, the Su Pedrosu Quarry has become part of a broader discussion on sustainable resource use. In recent years, the site has undergone environmental assessments for the installation of a non-hazardous waste recovery facility, aimed at recycling construction and demolition waste into secondary aggregates. This initiative reflects a shift toward a circular economy model but has required careful regulatory oversight. Environmental concerns focus on landscape alteration, groundwater protection, and dust and noise emissions in a predominantly rural setting. Additional sensitivity arises from the proximity of important archaeological sites, including prehistoric Domus de Janas tombs and Bronze Age Nuragic archeological remains.

The Rocca San Silvestro mining area is a remarkably well-preserved medieval mining and fortified settlement located near Campiglia Marittima in Tuscany, Italy. Founded in the early eleventh century by the Della Gherardesca family, the village flourished for roughly three centuries as a strategic center for the extraction and smelting of copper, lead, and silver, metals that supplied regional economies and coin mints such as those of Pisa and Lucca. Built on a limestone hill, Rocca San Silvestro combined military defense with industrial production and featured a planned layout with residential quarters, workshops, furnaces, and a castle. Abandoned in the fourteenth century due to economic changes, conflict, and shifting mining technologies, the site today forms part of the San Silvestro Archaeological Mines Park and is a landmark of medieval industrial archaeology. Geologically, the area lies within the Colline Metallifere, a district long known for polymetallic mineralization. Ore processing generated extensive slag heaps composed of glassy, iron-rich waste dominated by fayalite and wüstite. Over centuries, interaction with air and water produced a suite of secondary minerals within slag vesicles, making the site famous among mineralogists. These include rare species such as connellite and spangolite, along with native copper and native lead, anglesite, brochantite, cuprite, and malachite. Beyond its mineralogical interest, Rocca San Silvestro offers an exceptional opportunity for archaeometallurgy. The well-dated medieval slag deposits allow researchers to reconstruct smelting technologies, evaluate metallurgical efficiency, and trace ore sources through geochemical fingerprinting. Recent studies also use the site to investigate preindustrial health and environmental management, examining how medieval communities organized space to limit exposure to toxic metals. While contamination, structural instability, and conservation pressures pose challenges, the site remains a unique open-air laboratory for understanding the technological, environmental, and social dimensions of medieval mining and metallurgy.

Site: Province of Massa and Carrara, Tuscany
metadata: The Carrara marble quarries, located in the Apuan Alps of northern Tuscany near the city of Carrara, represent one of the most important and long-lived stone extraction districts in the world. Known to the Romans as marmor lunense (Luni marble), Carrara marble has been quarried for more than two millennia and has shaped global art, architecture, and culture. Its pure white statuario variety was prized for monumental sculpture, while other grades were widely used in architecture and decoration. From Roman imperial building programs through the Renaissance—when artists such as Michelangelo personally selected blocks—to modern construction and design, Carrara has produced more marble than any other quarrying region on Earth. Geologically, Carrara marble formed through the metamorphism of Jurassic limestone during the Alpine orogeny. Heat and pressure recrystallized the original carbonate sediment into a fine-grained mosaic of calcite crystals, giving the stone its strength, ability to take a high polish, and ability to preserve extremely fine detail. Variations in color and veining reflect minor impurities and structural deformation, producing well-known varieties such as Bianco Carrara, Statuario, Calacatta, Arabescato, and Bardiglio. A distinctive feature of Carrara’s history is its deep-rooted anarchist tradition. During the nineteenth century, quarry work was extremely dangerous, poorly paid, and largely unregulated. These harsh conditions fostered radical political ideas among the quarry workers. By the late 1800s, Carrara had become one of the main centers of anarchism in Italy, attracting exiled revolutionaries from across Europe. Workers organized mutual aid societies, strikes, and protests, most notably during the Lunigiana revolt of 1894, which was violently suppressed by the state. This anarchist identity became part of local culture and is still commemorated today through monuments, archives, and festivals. Quarrying remains hazardous despite advanced technology, and fatal accidents still occur. Environmental conflicts focus on mountain destruction, water pollution from marble dust, and the large-scale conversion of marble into calcium carbonate for industrial fillers rather than artistic use. These issues highlight enduring tensions between heritage, labor, environmental protection, and global industrial demand.

Site: Pomaia, Marmolaio Valley, Santa Luce, Pisa Province, Tuscany
metadata: The Serra Antica alabaster quarries are located in the Marmolaio Valley, about 1.5 kilometres south-west of Pomaia in the municipality of Santa Luce, Tuscany. This area has long been one of the most important extraction zones supplying the famous alabaster workshops of Volterra, which transformed the raw stone into objects traded across Europe and beyond. Quarrying in the district dates back to at least the Etruscan period, when alabaster was widely used for funerary urns and monuments. Activity declined in the Middle Ages but revived strongly from the sixteenth century onward, reaching a peak in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, when hundreds of quarrymen worked underground in the hills of Santa Luce and Castellina Marittima. Although large-scale extraction has largely ceased, the quarries remain a key element of Tuscany’s mining heritage. Geologically, the alabaster occurs within the Gessoso-solfifera Formation of Upper Miocene age. It formed around six million years ago during the Messinian salinity crisis, when partial evaporation of the Mediterranean Sea led to the precipitation of thick gypsum deposits. The alabaster is a fine-grained variety of gypsum (calcium sulphate) and is found not as continuous layers but as nodules of variable size—ranging from a few kilograms to several hundred kilograms, embedded within marls and clays. The Serra Antica district is especially valued for producing highly translucent, milk-white alabaster known as Scaglione. Additional issues and controversies relate mainly to safety and environmental management. Historically, underground mining using black powder and later explosives caused frequent tunnel collapses and serious health problems for miners. Today, debates focus on stabilising abandoned galleries, preserving them as industrial archaeology, and managing hydrogeological risks to the Marmolaio torrent.

The Allori Mine, near the Bomba locality in the municipality of Cavriglia (Arezzo Province, Tuscany), was a major open-pit lignite operation within the Santa Barbara mining concession and a key element of the Upper Valdarno mining district. Although coal mining in Cavriglia began in the nineteenth century, the Allori deposit became a focal point during the 1950s and 1960s, at the height of Italy’s lignite rush. The material extracted was xyloid lignite, a relatively young brown coal with high water content, but strategically important for power generation during the autarkic period and post-war reconstruction. In the 1960s, operations were taken over by the state-owned energy company Ente Nazionale per l’Energia Elettrica, and the mine supplied fuel directly to the Santa Barbara thermoelectric power plant, purpose-built to burn this low-quality lignite. Open-pit mining at Allori operated from 1974 to 1992, reaching a depth of about 150 metres, and involved the movement of millions of cubic metres of earth. After closure, the site was progressively reclaimed. Geologically, the Allori deposit lies within the Upper Valdarno Plio-Pleistocene lacustrine basin, composed of clays, silts, and organic-rich layers. The lignite formed from swamp and forest environments surrounding an ancient lake. The associated Meleto Clays are notable for hosting secondary minerals formed through oxidation and combustion processes, making the site well known among mineralogists. Beyond energy production, the mine is famous for mineral discoveries such as vivianite, anapaite, native sulfur, gypsum, and hematite, often formed in cavities and altered zones linked to lignite burning. Controversies centre on the profound landscape transformation that created lunar-like landscapes, the complete destruction of historic hamlets such as San Martino, and the displacement of entire communities. Although large-scale environmental reclamation has reshaped the former pit into rehabilitated land and artificial lakes, critics argue that the process has been uneven and largely cosmetic, and that the long-term chemical stability of the massive waste dumps—along with their potential impact on soils and groundwater—remains unresolved.

The Haile Gold Mine, located near Kershaw in Lancaster County, South Carolina, is the largest active gold mine on the eastern side of the United States and one of the oldest gold mining sites in the country. Gold was first discovered there in 1827, decades before the California Gold Rush, during the early nineteenth-century gold boom in the Carolinas. Since then, Haile has been mined intermittently using successive generations of technology, from panning and stamp mills to modern industrial methods. Cumulatively, the site has produced an estimated 11 metric tonnes of gold. After long periods of closure caused by industrial accidents, and low gold prices, the mine was redeveloped in the twenty-first century. OceanaGold Corporation acquired the property in 2015 and restarted production in 2017. Today, Haile operates as both an open-pit and underground mine, producing roughly 4 to 6 metric tonnes of gold per year, making it a major economic driver for the rural Kershaw area. Geologically, the mine lies within the Carolina Terrane, a northeast-trending geological belt that hosted the first gold rush in the United States. Gold mineralization occurs in laminated siltstones and volcanic rocks of the Upper Persimmon Fork Formation, dated to approximately 549 million years.. The deposit is classified as a low-sulfide disseminated gold system, with gold associated mainly with pyrite, pyrrhotite, and hydrothermal breccias. Modern ore processing uses crushing, grinding, flotation, and Carbon-In-Leach technology to efficiently recover gold and silver from relatively low-grade ore. Despite its economic importance, Haile remains controversial. Since reopening, the mine has faced environmental violations, including repeated exceedances of legal limits for cyanide, mercury, cadmium, and thallium in wastewater discharges, resulting in substantial state fines. Local residents and environmental groups have raised concerns about groundwater contamination, toxic pit lakes, wetland and stream impacts, noise, and long-term environmental risks, particularly in a region dependent on local water resources.
